funFacts: The 2-Litre used an engine design that dated back to 1919.
It featured an aluminum body over a wood frame, a traditional British construction method.
Initially available only as a sedan, a drophead coupe was added in 1949.
The Ace provided the chassis and body for the legendary Shelby Cobra.
It featured a lightweight ladder-frame chassis and independent transverse leaf-spring suspension.
The design was heavily inspired by the Ferrari 166 MM Barchetta.
Designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro in collaboration with the Centro Stile Alfa Romeo.
It features a distinctive 'triple headlight' design that became an Alfa Romeo signature of the 2000s.
The 159 shared its GM/Fiat Premium platform with the Brera and Spider.
The car uses a one-piece carbon fiber monocoque that weighs only 143 lbs (65 kg).
To save weight and maintain a raw feel, it has no power steering.
The '4C' stands for 4 cilindri (four cylinders) in Italian.
Only 500 coupes and 500 spiders were ever produced.
The name '8C' refers to the 8-cylinder engines of the 1930s and 40s.
Its chassis is based on a shortened version of the Maserati GranTurismo.
The modern Giulia Quadrifoglio was developed by engineers who previously worked on the Ferrari 458 Speciale.
It features an 'Active Aero Splitter' that lowers at high speeds to increase downforce.
The original 1960s Giulia was one of the first mainstream cars to use a wind tunnel for design.
The design was one of Giugiaro's first major projects while working for Bertone.
The 1750 and 2000 GTV models are considered among the best-handling cars of their era.
It featured the iconic 'Scalino' or 'step-front' hood design on early models.
The 1950s Giulietta Sprint was so popular it single-handedly changed Alfa Romeo from a niche maker to a high-volume manufacturer.
The 2010 hatchback version used a 'DNA' selector to change engine and steering response.
It was named 'Giulietta' as a play on 'Romeo and Juliet'.
It is named after the Stelvio Pass, one of the most famous driving roads in the Italian Alps.
The Stelvio Quadrifoglio held the record for the fastest production SUV around the Nürburgring upon its release.
It uses a carbon fiber driveshaft as standard across all trim levels.
The original A110 was a rally legend, winning the first-ever World Rally Championship in 1973.
The modern A110 is built almost entirely of aluminum to keep its weight around 1,100 kg.
Its unique 4-light front design is a direct tribute to the original rally car's lighting setup.
The 'S' stands for Sport, featuring stiffer springs and anti-roll bars than the standard A110.
It is one of the few modern cars that can be ordered with factory-fitted Michelin Pilot Sport Cup 2 tires.
The 'Orange Feu' (Fire Orange) color is exclusive to the S and GT models.
The Gremlin was designed by drawing on an air-sickness bag during a flight.
It was launched on April Fool's Day in 1970.
Despite its strange 'chopped off' rear design, it was a massive sales success for AMC.
A Hornet famously performed a 360-degree 'corkscrew' jump in the James Bond film 'The Man with the Golden Gun'.
The Hornet platform was used as the basis for the Gremlin, Spirit, Concord, and Eagle.
It was one of the first American compacts to offer a performance-oriented 'SC/360' version.
The Javelin was the only pony car used as a standard-issue highway patrol car in the US (Alabama).
The second generation featured prominent 'humps' over the front wheels, inspired by the Corvette.
It was very successful in Trans-Am racing, winning championships in 1971 and 1972.
The Atom has no doors, no roof, and no windshield, using an exoskeleton chassis for structure.
It became world-famous after Jeremy Clarkson's face was visibly distorted by its speed on Top Gear.
The Atom 500 version features a custom-built V8 made from two Suzuki motorcycle engines.
The DB11 was the first Aston Martin to use an engine developed in partnership with Mercedes-AMG.
It features an 'AeroBlade' system that channels air through the C-pillars and out of a slit in the trunk to create downforce.
Its clamshell hood is one of the largest single-piece aluminum pressings in the automotive world.
AMR replaced the standard DB11 V12, adding more power and a louder exhaust note.
It can be identified by its exposed carbon fiber trim and lime green brake calipers.
The top speed was increased to 208 mph, making it one of the world's fastest GT cars.
The DB5 is the most famous movie car in history, debuting in the James Bond film 'Goldfinger'.
The 'DB' initials stand for David Brown, the owner of Aston Martin for 25 years.
It was the first Aston Martin to use the ZF five-speed transmission as standard.
The name 'Superleggera' is a tribute to the Italian coachbuilder Touring, who helped design the DB4.
It produces 180 kg of downforce at its top speed, the most for any series-production Aston Martin GT.
The engine produces a massive 663 lb-ft of torque starting at just 1,800 RPM.
The DBX is the first-ever SUV built by Aston Martin in its 100+ year history.
It features a unique 'ducktail' rear spoiler inspired by the Aston Martin Vantage.
Each DBX is hand-stitched and takes over 200 hours to complete.
The 'Vantage' name was historically used to denote high-performance versions of other models before becoming its own model.
The 1970s V8 Vantage was once called 'Britain's First Supercar' because it was faster than a Ferrari Daytona.
The current Vantage serves as the Official Safety Car for Formula 1.
The Vulcan is a track-only hypercar; only 24 were ever built.
The engine produces 820 horsepower without the use of turbochargers or superchargers.
Owners had to undergo a mandatory driver training program before being allowed to take delivery of the car.
The Audi 80 formed the basis for the first-generation Volkswagen Passat.
The 1986 'B3' generation featured the 'Procon-ten' safety system, which pulled the steering wheel away from the driver in a crash.
It was the first Audi model to offer the legendary Quattro all-wheel-drive system in a mainstream sedan.
The Quattro was the first rally car to take advantage of the rules allowing all-wheel drive.
It features a unique 5-cylinder engine note that became the soundtrack of 1980s rallying.
The name 'Ur-Quattro' is used by fans, where 'Ur-' is German for 'original'.
The R8 shares its engine and chassis architecture with the Lamborghini Gallardo and Huracán.
It was the first production car in the world to feature full-LED headlights (2008).
Audi built an all-electric 'R8 e-tron' version that was featured in the movie 'Iron Man'.
The first generation featured signature 'Sideblades'—air intake covers that could be ordered in contrasting colors.
It features a gated manual shifter on early V8 models, which is now a highly desirable collector's item.
Audi's Quattro system in the R8 is rear-biased, sending up to 85% of power to the rear wheels in normal driving.
The Spyder replaces the 'Sideblades' with more traditional air intakes to accommodate the folding roof mechanism.
It features a power-operated glass rear window that can be lowered even with the roof up to hear the V10 better.
The microphones for the Bluetooth hands-free system are built directly into the seatbelts.
The RS 2 was co-developed with Porsche and was built at Porsche's Rossle-Bau plant in Stuttgart.
It uses Porsche-branded Brembo brakes, Porsche side mirrors, and Porsche wheels.
It was the first Audi 'RS' model and established the tradition of high-performance Audi station wagons.
The RS 3's 5-cylinder engine follows a 1-2-4-5-3 firing order, giving it a sound similar to original rally cars.
The current generation features an 'RS Torque Splitter' that allows for controlled drifts.
It is the only car in its class (premium compact) to still offer a 5-cylinder engine.
The second generation (C6) featured a 5.0L Twin-Turbo V10 derived from the Lamborghini Gallardo.
The current model features a 48V mild-hybrid system to help improve fuel efficiency during city driving.
It is one of the widest cars in its class, with aggressive flared wheel arches that are unique to the RS model.
The S1 is the smallest Quattro-equipped Audi ever built.
It uses the name of the legendary Group B Sport Quattro S1 rally car.
Unlike the standard A1, the S1 features a complex multi-link rear suspension to accommodate the all-wheel-drive system.
Early S4s were based on the Audi 100, but since 1997, the S4 has been the performance version of the A4.
The B5 generation (1997-2002) is a cult favorite due to its highly tunable 2.7L Twin-Turbo V6.
The B6 and B7 generations famously shoehorned a 4.2L V8 into the compact A4 chassis.
In many European markets, the S4 Avant outsells the S4 Sedan due to its practicality.
The B8 generation (2009-2016) used a supercharged V6, despite still wearing 'V6T' badges (which usually mean turbo).
It features Audi's 'Torsen' center differential, providing permanent, mechanical all-wheel drive.
The TT is named after the 'Tourist Trophy' motorcycle race on the Isle of Man.
The first generation's design was so clean it is considered a landmark of industrial design.
Early models were recalled to add a rear spoiler after several high-speed accidents in Germany.
The TT RS was developed by Audi's high-performance subsidiary, Audi Sport GmbH (formerly quattro GmbH).
Its 5-cylinder engine has won 'International Engine of the Year' in its category for nine consecutive years.
It features a fixed rear wing and massive oval exhaust tips, signatures of the Audi RS family.
Known as the 'Big Healey' to distinguish it from the smaller Sprite model.
It was a very successful rally car, frequently winning events like the Alpine Rally in the early 60s.
The 3000 MkIII featured a luxurious walnut-veneer dashboard and roll-up windows, a big step up from earlier models.
The Continental GT was the first Bentley to be developed under Volkswagen Group ownership.
The W12 engine is essentially two V6 engines joined at the base, making it extremely compact.
The interior uses 'bull hide' leather from cows raised in high-altitude regions to ensure there are no insect bite marks.
At launch, it was the only car in the compact hatchback segment to feature rear-wheel drive.
The 1 Series was the spiritual successor to the BMW 2002 from the 1960s.
The design was led by Chris Bangle and featured 'flame surfacing' lines.
It was not named the 'M1' because that name was already used for BMW's 1970s supercar.
It was only available with a 6-speed manual transmission.
It features the wider suspension and brakes from the E92 M3.
The 2 Series replaced the 1 Series Coupe and Convertible models.
While the 1 Series hatchback moved to front-wheel drive, the 2 Series Coupe remains rear-wheel drive.
The M240i model is often praised as a 'baby M2' for its incredible performance-to-price ratio.
The 2002 is the car that saved BMW from financial ruin and established its reputation in the US.
The '2002' name comes from the 2.0-liter engine and the fact that it was a 2-door car.
The 2002 Turbo (1973) was the first turbocharged production car in Europe.
The 3 Series is BMW's best-selling model, accounting for about 30% of the brand's total annual sales.
It is the most successful touring car in the world, with thousands of racing victories.
The '3 Series' name has covered seven different generations of cars since 1975.
The E30 introduced the first 4-door body style to the 3 Series range.
It was the first 3 Series to offer a diesel engine and all-wheel drive (the 325iX).
The E30 is widely considered the 'gold standard' for modern classic enthusiast cars.
The E36 introduced the 'Z-axle' multi-link rear suspension which significantly improved handling.
It was the first 3 Series to feature the 'Hatchback' body style (the 3 Series Compact).
The E36 M3 was the first M3 to use a straight-six engine instead of a four-cylinder.
Many enthusiasts consider the E46 the peak of BMW design and engineering balance.
It featured the 'Steptronic' semi-automatic transmission and curtain airbags for the first time.
The E46 M3 CSL (Coupe Sport Lightweight) is one of the most legendary driver's cars ever made.
The E90 introduced run-flat tires as standard to the 3 Series, a controversial move among enthusiasts.
It was the first 3 Series to feature a turbocharged gasoline engine (the 335i).
The wagon version (E91) is a cult favorite in the US where it was sold in limited numbers.
This generation saw the switch from hydraulic power steering to electric power steering.
It was the first 3 Series to be offered with a hybrid powertrain (the ActiveHybrid 3).
The F30 generation split the coupe and convertible into the separate 4 Series line.
The G20 is 55 kg lighter than the previous generation despite being larger.
It features 'Reversing Assistant,' which can automatically steer the car back the way it came for up to 50 meters.
The drag coefficient is as low as 0.23, making it one of the most aerodynamic cars in its class.
The GT features a wheelbase 110mm longer than the sedan, providing more rear legroom than a 5 Series.
It is the only 3 Series to feature an active rear spoiler that rises automatically at high speeds.
It was designed as a niche alternative for people who wanted the space of a wagon but the look of a coupe.
The E9 chassis was the basis for the legendary 'Batmobile' 3.0 CSL racing car.
Its pillarless side profile and slim roof pillars offer exceptional 360-degree visibility.
It established the 'luxury performance' identity that BMW would follow for the next 50 years.
Known for its exceptionally smooth small-displacement 6-cylinder engine.
It was a status symbol of the 1980s 'Yuppie' era in both Europe and the USA.
The E30 320i was famously used in the movie 'The Wolf of Wall Street' (as Jordan Belfort's early car).
The flagship model of the original 3 Series, featuring fuel injection and four-wheel disc brakes.
Identifiable by its dual exhaust pipes, one on each side of the rear bumper.
It was one of the fastest small sedans in the world when it was released.
Won the 1940 Mille Miglia race with an average speed of 103 mph.
Only 464 units were ever built, making it one of the rarest pre-war BMWs.
It featured a tubular frame and independent front suspension, which was very advanced for the 1930s.
Separated from the 3 Series name to distinguish coupes and convertibles as a more premium line.
The current generation (G22) introduced a controversial vertical 'beaver tooth' kidney grille.
It features a significantly lower center of gravity compared to the 3 Series sedan for better cornering.

engine: Various I4, I6, and V8 options
funFacts: The 5 Series was the car that introduced the 'Series' naming system to BMW's lineup.
It is famously used as the benchmark for the 'Executive Sedan' segment worldwide.
BMW has produced over 8 million 5 Series cars since its inception.
The E28 generation was the first to offer a diesel engine in a BMW (the 524td).
It introduced anti-lock brakes (ABS) to the 5 Series for the first time.
This generation saw the creation of the first-ever BMW M5.
The design by Chris Bangle was initially polarizing but is now seen as a design turning point for BMW.
It was the first 5 Series to use the iDrive control system.
The M5 version features a V10 engine that was developed using BMW's Formula 1 racing expertise.

engine: Various Turbocharged I4, I6, and V8s
funFacts: Marked a return to more conservative styling after the experimental E60 generation.
It was the first 5 Series to offer an 8-speed automatic transmission.
The F10 M5 switched from the V10 to a twin-turbocharged V8 for better low-end torque.
Despite the 'Coupe' name, it is a 4-door car with a low-slung, sleek roofline.
It was BMW's response to the popular Mercedes-Benz CLS 'four-door coupe' trend.
The rear center seat is very small, often referred to as a '4+1' seating arrangement.

engine: Various Inline-6, V8, and V12 options
funFacts: BMW's flagship model, used to showcase their most expensive and cutting-edge tech.
The 7 Series was the first car in the world to offer a built-in GPS navigation system.
It is the favorite choice for heads of state and business executives in Europe.
Considered by many fans to be the most beautiful luxury sedan ever built by BMW.
It gained fame in the film 'Tomorrow Never Dies' where James Bond drove it by remote control.
It was the first European car to be offered with an integrated satellite navigation system.
The entire passenger cell is made from carbon fiber to keep the car light despite heavy batteries.
It uses 'laser headlights', which are 1,000 times brighter than standard LEDs.
The futuristic design looks almost identical to the initial concept car shown in 2011.
The M3 was originally born as a racing car that happened to be legal for the street.
It is the most decorated touring car in the world, having won titles in almost every series it entered.
Every generation of M3 is the benchmark that every other performance sedan is measured against.
Every body panel except the hood and roof is different from a standard E30 to improve aerodynamics.
The rear window glass was re-angled and the trunk lid raised to help air flow better to the rear wing.
It is the only M3 to use a 4-cylinder engine, designed for high-RPM racing performance.
The only M3 in history to come from the factory with a V8 engine.
The engine revs to a staggering 8,400 RPM, producing a sound like a baby Formula 1 car.
The carbon fiber roof was a standard feature on the coupe to lower the center of gravity.
The first M3 to offer a factory All-Wheel Drive system (M xDrive).
It features a 'Drift Analyzer' that gives the driver a 5-star rating based on the angle and distance of their drift.
The engine is so strong that its basic block is used in BMW's M4 GT3 race cars.
Fans waited over 35 years for BMW to finally build a production M3 wagon.
It set a record as the fastest station wagon around the Nürburgring upon its release.
It is only available in the top-tier 'Competition' trim with 503 horsepower.
The M4 replaced the M3 Coupe when BMW changed its naming system in 2014.
The limited-edition M4 CSL (2022) removed the rear seats to save weight and maximize track performance.
It uses a single-piece carbon fiber driveshaft to reduce rotational mass and improve throttle response.
This was the first M4 and the first mid-size M-car to move away from natural aspiration to turbos.
It features an 'Active M Differential' that can vary the locking effect between the rear wheels in milliseconds.
The hood is made from aluminum and features a 'power bulge' to accommodate the engine's charge air cooler.

engine: Various options up to a Hybrid V8
funFacts: The M5 is generally considered the car that invented the 'super sedan' category.
Early M5s were hand-built by BMW M technicians in Garching, Germany.
Modern M5s are capable of reaching 60 mph faster than many Lamborghinis and Ferraris.
It was the last hand-assembled M-car and the last to use the legendary S38 racing-derived engine.
Early models had 'M-System' wheels that looked like fans, designed to pull air into the brakes.
A limited number of 20 'Elekta' editions were built specifically for the Italian market.
Features a carbon fiber roof and a massive amount of leather and luxury inside.
It shares its engine and 7-speed dual-clutch transmission with the F10 M5.
Despite being a heavy grand tourer, it could reach a top speed of 190 mph with the M Driver's Package.
The X1 was BMW's entry into the subcompact luxury crossover market.
The first generation was built on the 3 Series Touring chassis, making it a very sporty handler.
The latest models are built on a front-wheel-drive platform shared with MINI models.
This generation moved the X1 to a transverse engine layout to provide significantly more interior space.
It was the first BMW SUV to be based on a front-wheel-drive architecture.
It proved so popular that it briefly became one of BMW's top three best-selling models worldwide.
BMW's first SUV; it was famously designed while BMW owned Land Rover.
It was marketed as a 'Sport Activity Vehicle' to distance it from slower, clunky traditional SUVs.
The 4.6is and 4.8is models were the predecessors to the modern high-performance X5 M.
This generation introduced the first plug-in hybrid X5, the xDrive40e.
It featured 'Aero Curtains' in the front bumper that reduced fuel consumption by channeling air around the wheels.
It was the first X5 to offer a digital instrument cluster in later production years.
Pioneered the 'SUV Coupe' segment, which has since been copied by almost every other luxury brand.
The X6 M (along with the X5 M) was the first M-car to use an automatic transmission and all-wheel drive.
The current version has 617 horsepower, making it as powerful as a McLaren F1.
The current generation (G29) was co-developed with the Toyota GR Supra.
The design of the first generation was led by Chris Bangle and featured sharp, 'flame surfaced' lines.
BMW moved from a folding hardtop back to a lightweight fabric soft-top for the latest model to save weight.
The Isabella was the most successful model for the German brand Borgward before they went bankrupt in 1961.
The car's name was originally a secret project code, but the public loved it so much that Borgward kept it.
It was considered more stylish and modern than many equivalent Mercedes-Benz models of the mid-50s.
The Chiron's engine has 10 radiators to manage the extreme heat produced by its four turbochargers.
At its top speed of 261 mph, the tires will only last for about 15 minutes of continuous driving.
Its headlights are the flattest LED projectors ever fitted to a car, each containing eight individual light squares.
Replaced the Buick Wildcat as the brand's 'sporty' full-size car.
The name was taken from a 1956 concept car that featured a revolutionary backup camera.
It used a unique Roman soldier's head emblem instead of the usual Buick Trishield logo.
The '225' refers to the car's overall length in inches—nearly 19 feet long.
It was nicknamed 'Deuce and a Quarter' by American car enthusiasts.
It was the absolute flagship of the Buick range, offering Cadillac-levels of luxury for a slightly lower price.
The GS (Gran Sport) was Buick's equivalent to the legendary Pontiac GTO muscle car.
The 1970 Stage 1 455 version produced 510 lb-ft of torque, a record that stood for 33 years in America.
Known as 'gentleman's muscle cars' because they combined raw speed with very high-quality interior materials.

engine: Various I6, V6, and V8 options
funFacts: For many decades, the LeSabre was Buick's entry-level full-size car and its most consistent best-seller.
The name was inspired by the 1951 Le Sabre concept car, which looked like a jet plane for the road.
In the 1990s, the LeSabre was consistently ranked as one of the most reliable American cars on the market.
The 1958 model is famous for its excessive chrome, including 16 square blocks on the rear fenders.
In the 30s, the Limited was so luxurious that Cadillac executives reportedly complained it was taking their customers.
The 1958 Limited was only built for one year due to its high price and the US recession of that year.
The second-gen Regal was the platform for the legendary 'Grand National' and 'GNX' muscle cars.
The Regal nameplate has been more successful in China than in its home market of the USA.
The final generation was a rebadged version of the German-engineered Opel Insignia.
The 1963 Riviera is considered by many designers to be the most beautiful American car ever made.
The 1971-73 models are famous for their 'Boattail' rear window design.
It was GM's first purpose-built entry into the 'personal luxury car' market.
The original 1953 model featured chrome-plated wire wheels as standard.
The 1960s Skylark was the basis for the high-performance Gran Sport models.
The 1990s Skylark featured a very controversial 'beak' nose that made it easy to identify from a distance.
The 1961 Special introduced an aluminum V8 engine that was eventually sold to Rover and used in Land Rovers for 40 years.
Traditionally the most affordable car in the Buick range.
The 1949 model featured the first 'VentiPorts' on the fenders, which became a permanent Buick design trait.
Named after a line of jet-powered experimental concept cars from the early 1950s.
It featured a console-mounted tachometer, a very sporty feature for a full-size luxury car in the 60s.
The 1970 model was the final year and is prized for its massive 455 cubic-inch V8 engine.
One of the last Cadillacs to use traditional body-on-frame construction and a rear-wheel-drive layout.
Its name is a reference to a type of horse-drawn carriage used in the 1800s.
It was a favorite for luxury limo conversions due to its massive, square proportions.
The 1959 model is world-famous for having the tallest and most iconic tailfins in automotive history.
The name 'DeVille' is French for 'of the town' or 'urban'.
In 2000, it was the first production car in the world to offer a Night Vision infrared system.
The 1970 model used a massive 500 cubic-inch (8.2L) V8, the highest displacement engine in any post-WWII car.
The 1967 model moved to front-wheel drive, using a specialized 'Unified Powerplant Package' to handle the V8's power.
The 1953 original was a limited edition halo car with only 532 units ever built.
The vehicle of choice for US Presidents and foreign dignitaries throughout the 1980s.
It featured extra-thick carpeting and deep-tufted leather seats that felt like a living room sofa.
The 'Fleetwood' name came from an old-school high-end coachbuilding company in Pennsylvania.
It was the first Cadillac to feature 'Dagmar' bumpers—the large bullet-shaped guards on the front.
The 1948 model introduced the tailfin, a design trend that would define the entire 1950s era of American cars.
It was the primary high-volume seller that established Cadillac as the 'Standard of the World'.
The middle model between the basic 150 and the luxury Bel Air.
Hot rodders loved the 210 because it was lighter and simpler than the Bel Air.
It is a core member of the legendary 'Tri-Five' Chevrolet family (1955, 1956, 1957).
Built in Argentina, it was based on the Chevy II/Nova but adapted for South American roads.
It was a dominant force in Argentine touring car racing (Turismo Carretera) for many years.
It is a beloved classic in Argentina, much like the original Mustang is in the USA.
The 1957 Bel Air is arguably the most recognizable American car design of all time.
In 1955, it debuted the legendary Small Block V8 engine, which remained in production for decades.
It started as a luxury trim of the Deluxe model before becoming its own legendary series.
The 'Nomad' was the high-end, 2-door sporty station wagon variant of the Bel Air.
1955-1957 models are prized by collectors for their 'pillarless' design on the Nomad sub-model.
It was one of the first wagons to transition from a utility vehicle to a status symbol for suburban families.
The Biscayne was the least expensive model in Chevrolet's full-size car range.
Because it was the lightest full-size model and lacked heavy trim, it was a favorite for drag racers who wanted the 'big block' engines.
Most Biscaynes were sold with very few options, often lacking power steering or even a clock.
The 'C' in C10 stands for conventional (rear-wheel drive), while 'K' designated four-wheel drive.
The 1967–1972 generation is often called 'Action Line' and is considered one of the most beautiful trucks ever designed.
It was the first major pickup series to introduce independent front suspension for a more car-like ride.
The name was allegedly chosen because Chevrolet said it meant 'a small, vicious animal that eats Mustangs'.
The Camaro was a direct response to the success of the Ford Mustang.
It has served as the Official Pace Car for the Indianapolis 500 more than nine times.
The 'Exorcist' package was designed by Hennessey Performance to 'banish' the Dodge Challenger Demon.
In its highest trim, it produces 1,000 horsepower and 883 lb-ft of torque.
Despite its massive power, Hennessey offers a 2-year/24,000-mile warranty on the conversion.
The RS (Rally Sport) is an appearance package that could be added to any model, including the SS or Z/28.
On classic 1967-1969 models, the RS package included the iconic hidden headlights behind vacuum-operated doors.
The RS trim focused on styling, including upgraded lighting, trim, and badging.
This represents a car ordered with both the Rally Sport (appearance) and Super Sport (performance) packages.
It is easily identified by having the SS performance hood and badging combined with the RS hidden headlights.
The RS/SS 396 convertible from 1969 is one of the most sought-after collector Camaros in existence.
The SS was the high-performance package that introduced big-block V8 power to the pony car market.
Early SS models featured a signature 'ice cube tray' simulated vents on the hood.
Modern SS models feature 'Magnetic Ride Control', a suspension technology also used by Ferrari.
The '396' refers to the displacement in cubic inches, making it a 'Big Block' muscle car.
In 1969, the L78 version of this engine produced a massive 375 horsepower.
Because of the weight of the big-block engine, these cars were known more for straight-line speed than cornering.
A common enthusiast reversal of the RS/SS name; it signifies a performance model with the aesthetics of the Rally Sport.
Hidden headlights on these models required a complex system of vacuum hoses and actuators to operate.
1969 models with this combo featured unique rear fender 'gills' ahead of the rear wheels.
The Z/28 was originally an RPO (Regular Production Option) code for a race-ready engine designed for the SCCA Trans-Am series.
The 2014-2015 Z/28 was famously track-focused, even deleting the air conditioning and all but one speaker to save weight.
Unlike the SS, the Z/28 was traditionally designed for superior handling on a road course rather than just drag racing.
The original 1969 ZL1 featured an all-aluminum racing engine; only 69 units were ever built.
The modern ZL1 uses the same supercharged 'LT4' engine found in the Corvette Z06.
It features 11 heat exchangers to keep the engine, transmission, and differential cool during track use.
The Caprice was Chevrolet's most luxurious and expensive full-size car, positioned above the Impala.
The 1991–1996 generation was famous for its 'whale' or 'bubble' body shape.
Because of its durable frame and powerful V8, it was the most popular police interceptor in America for decades.
The LS was the top-of-the-line luxury trim, often featuring a formal vinyl roof and plush 'velour' interiors.
It represented the absolute peak of standard American full-size luxury before the move to front-wheel drive.
It was frequently used as the basis for high-end brougham-style custom conversions.
The Chevelle was one of Chevrolet's most successful nameplates, filling the gap between the small Nova and the large Impala.
It was built on GM's A-body platform, which was shared with the Pontiac GTO and Oldsmobile 442.
The Chevelle Lagoon (1973-1976) was the high-end luxury version of the final generation.
The 1970 Chevelle SS 454 is widely considered the king of the muscle car era.
The LS6 engine option in 1970 produced an underrated 450 horsepower, a massive number for the time.
It was the first muscle car to offer 'Cowl Induction', a system that used a flapper door in the hood to draw in cool air.
In 1968, the SS 396 became its own separate model series rather than just an option package.
The engine actually measured 402 cubic inches in 1970, but Chevrolet kept the 396 name because of its brand recognition.
It was the car that brought big-block performance to the masses at an affordable price.

engine: Various I4, I6, and V8 options
funFacts: The car was officially named 'Chevy II', with 'Nova' being the name of the top-level trim.
It was designed in record time (only 18 months) to compete with the Ford Falcon.
Despite its simple economy-car origins, it became a legendary muscle car when V8s were shoehorned into its small chassis.
The Colorado replaced the long-running Chevrolet S-10 pickup.
The ZR2 trim is a factory-built off-roader with advanced DSSV dampers normally found in F1 cars.
In some international markets, it is sold as an Isuzu, following a partnership between GM and Isuzu.
The Confederate was the first Chevrolet to feature a built-in radiator grille shell.
It earned the nickname 'Baby Cadillac' because of its sophisticated styling and smooth 6-cylinder engine.
It was produced during the height of the Great Depression, yet sold over 300,000 units.
It is the only mass-produced American passenger car to feature a rear-mounted, air-cooled engine.
Ralph Nader's book 'Unsafe at Any Speed' famously targeted the Corvair's suspension design.
Despite its reputation, the Corvair was actually a very successful sports car in autocross and road racing.
The Corvette is known as 'America's Sports Car'.
For the first 66 years of its life, the engine was in the front, until the move to mid-engine in 2020.
Every generation of Corvette since 1953 has used a body made of composite materials like fiberglass or carbon fiber.
The first mid-engine Corvette to enter full production, fulfilling a dream held by its original designer, Zora Arkus-Duntov.
Because the engine is in the back, the C8 has two trunks (one in front, one in back).
It is the first Corvette to be offered without a manual transmission option from the factory.
The first-year 1953 models were only available in Polo White with a Red interior.
Early models were criticized for having an underpowered 6-cylinder engine and a 2-speed automatic transmission.
The iconic 'Quad Headlight' look was introduced in 1958.
1963 was the only year the 'Split Window' coupe was produced, which is now the most collectible version.
It introduced independent rear suspension, a massive technical leap for the Corvette.
The design was inspired by a Mako Shark caught by GM's head of design, Bill Mitchell.
The longest-running Corvette generation, staying in production for 15 years.
It popularized the 'T-Top' removable roof panels.
It featured vacuum-operated pop-up headlights that remained a Corvette staple for decades.
Introduced the legendary 'LS' family of engines that changed the aftermarket performance world.
It used a rear-mounted transaxle for perfect 50:50 weight distribution.
The Z06 version (2001) used a thinner windshield and titanium exhaust to save every possible ounce of weight.
It was the first Corvette since 1962 to feature exposed (non-pop-up) headlights.
The ZR1 variant (2009) featured a polycarbonate window in the hood to show off the supercharger.
The Z06 model used a massive 7.0-liter (427 cubic inch) engine, the largest Small Block in history.
The last generation of front-engine Corvettes.
It brought back the 'Stingray' nameplate for the first time since 1976.
The interior was a massive upgrade over the C6, using magnesium-frame seats and real carbon fiber trim.
The Z06 version features the most powerful naturally aspirated V8 engine ever put in a production car (670 hp).
Its chassis is so stiff that the convertible version doesn't require extra structural bracing.
It features a 'Front Lift' system that can remember GPS coordinates to automatically lift the nose for steep driveways.
The spelling was two words ('Sting Ray') in the 60s and one word ('Stingray') in the 70s and beyond.
The name was inspired by Bill Mitchell's XP-755 racing concept car.
In the C7 and C8 generations, 'Stingray' denotes the base-model V8 Corvette.
The El Camino SS was technically a car-based pickup truck (a 'Coupe Utility') with muscle car performance.
In 1970, you could order an El Camino with the same 450hp LS6 engine as the Chevelle SS.
Enthusiasts often call it 'The mullets of cars'—business in the front, party in the back.
Famous for its 'Fastback' or 'Aerosedan' styling where the roof slopes all the way to the rear bumper.
It was Chevrolet's top-selling model in the late 40s.
The design was extremely aerodynamic for its time, leading to its popularity in early custom car culture.

engine: Various I6, V6, and V8 options
funFacts: The Impala was once the best-selling car in the entire United States (1965).
The 1958 model featured a unique triple-taillight setup that became an Impala trademark.
The high-performance Impala SS of the 1990s is a cult classic for its menacing all-black appearance.
The Malibu started as a high-end trim level of the Chevrolet Chevelle before becoming its own model.
In the 1970s, it was a staple of NASCAR racing.
The modern Malibu is one of the few remaining traditional sedans offered by an American manufacturer.
Introduced as Chevrolet's first 'personal luxury car' to compete with the Ford Thunderbird.
The 1970–1972 models are famous for having the longest hood in Chevrolet history.
It was a dominant force in NASCAR during the 1980s and 2000s, driven by legends like Dale Earnhardt.
The urban legend that the Nova didn't sell in Mexico because 'no va' means 'it won't go' is completely false.
The 1968–1972 Nova is a favorite for drag racers due to its light weight and ability to swallow large V8 engines.
In the late 80s, the Nova name was briefly used on a subcompact car built in a joint venture with Toyota.
The Nova SS was one of the smallest and lightest muscle cars GM ever produced.
The 1968–1970 396-cubic-inch versions are often called 'Little Beasts' for their incredible power-to-weight ratio.
It was a sleeper car—it looked like an economy car but could beat much more expensive supercars in a race.
The Opala is a legendary car in Brazil, combining an Opel Rekord body with a Chevrolet Impala engine.
It was the first passenger car built by General Motors in Brazil.
The 4.1-liter straight-six 'Opala 250-S' is an icon of Brazilian performance culture.
The 'Caravan' was the station wagon version of the popular Opala.
It was used extensively as a family car, an ambulance, and even by the police in Brazil.
Because of its smooth six-cylinder power and rear-wheel drive, it remains a favorite for custom 'street wagon' builds.
This was the most refined and luxurious version of the early Opala series.
It featured exclusive trim, a vinyl-covered roof, and a much quieter, better-insulated interior.
It was replaced in 1975 by the 'Diplomata' trim level as the flagship of the range.
The Sail was the first modern family car to be designed and built in China for the Chinese market by a foreign joint venture.
It is based on the platform of the second-generation Opel Corsa.
It is sold extensively in emerging markets like India, South America, and Southeast Asia as a low-cost reliable sedan.
The name 'Silverado' was originally a trim level for the Chevrolet C/K trucks before becoming its own model.
It is consistently one of the two best-selling vehicles in the United States every year.
Modern Silverados are offered with an optional 'Multi-Flex Tailgate' that can fold into six different positions.
The Spark was originally a Daewoo design before GM bought the Korean company.
The 'Spark EV' was one of the fastest electric cars in its class, producing a surprising 400 lb-ft of torque.
It was famously used as the basis for the car in the film 'Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen' (the character 'Skids').
The Styleline featured 'ponton' styling where the front and rear fenders were integrated into the body line.
The 'Deluxe' trim was the top-tier offering, featuring more chrome and a better clock.
It was the first Chevrolet to offer a fully automatic transmission (the 'Powerglide') in 1950.
The original 1955 model was the most powerful production car in America at the time.
The 2005 reboot was built using many chassis components from the Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W211).
It popularized the 'Bentley-style' mesh grille on an affordable American sedan.
The 'S' stands for Sport, featuring blacked-out trim and a stiffer suspension compared to the standard 300.
It includes a unique 'Sport Mode' that sharpens the steering and gear shifts.
The 300S came standard with a BeatsAudio sound system, targeting a younger, more modern buyer.
For several decades, Imperial was its own separate luxury brand, intended to compete with Cadillac and Lincoln.
The 1955 Imperial featured the first-ever transistor radio in a production car.
The 1990s version was a luxury sedan that shared its platform with the Chrysler Fifth Avenue.
The car's marketing campaign used the simple catchphrase 'Hi!' to highlight its friendly, round headlights.
The Neon SRT-4 (Dodge) was one of the fastest turbocharged cars of the early 2000s.
It was the first small American car to use 'cab-forward' design to maximize interior space.
The design requirement was to be able to carry a basket of eggs across a plowed field without breaking any.
The engine is so simple it can be completely dismantled using only a handful of tools.
The roof is made of canvas and can be rolled all the way back to the rear bumper.
Famous for its 'Z-line' design with an inverted rear window that stays dry when it rains.
It was the first car in the world to feature rectangular headlights.
Despite its strange looks, it was the best-selling car in France for several years in the mid-1960s.
Considered one of the most technologically advanced cars ever made, featuring hydropneumatic self-leveling suspension.
The 1967 update introduced 'swiveling' headlights that turned with the steering wheel to look around corners.
It famously saved French President Charles de Gaulle from an assassination attempt by being able to drive on three wheels after a tire was shot out.
The 'SM' uses a high-performance engine developed by Maserati, which Citroën owned at the time.
It features a variable-assist power steering system ('DIRAVI') that automatically centers the wheels even when stationary.
It is one of the most aerodynamic cars of its era, with a drag coefficient of 0.26.
It is a Romanian-built development of the Renault 12.
It was the primary car for Romanian citizens during the communist era and remains a national icon.
The 1310 stayed in production for 25 years with only minor cosmetic updates.
The Solenza was the final bridge model before the launch of the Logan, which transformed Dacia into a global brand.
It shared many parts, including the engine and gearbox, with the second-generation Renault Clio.
It was the first Dacia model to offer modern amenities like air conditioning and power windows as options.
The Nexia was essentially a heavily facelifted version of the 1980s Opel Kadett E.
It was a massive success in Eastern Europe and Uzbekistan, where production continued until 2016.
It was one of the first cars to offer a significant amount of standard features for a very low entry price.
A 'Kei car' designed to meet Japan's strict size and engine displacement regulations.
It features a 'Miracle Open Door' where the B-pillar is built into the doors, creating a massive opening for easy entry.
The name 'Tanto' is Italian for 'so much', referencing the car's surprising amount of interior space.
The 'Custom' variant features aggressive styling, LED lighting, and more premium interior materials than the standard Tanto.
It is aimed at younger buyers who want a stylish, modified look from the factory.
In Japan, the Tanto Custom is often among the top 5 best-selling vehicles annually.
It is a mini SUV that offers legitimate off-road capability in a very small package.
The first generation was narrow enough to qualify as a Kei car in Japan if fitted with a smaller engine.
It is extremely popular in Southeast Asia, where it is often sold as the Toyota Rush with 7 seats.
The 1200 was the most fuel-efficient car in America in 1973 (28.7 mpg city / 37.5 mpg highway).
Because of its light weight and rev-happy engine, it became a legend in the world of mini-stock racing.
It was the first Datsun/Nissan model to use MacPherson strut front suspension.
The car that changed the world's perception of Japanese cars from 'cheap' to 'desirable sports cars'.
It was designed specifically for the US market by Yutaka Katayama, famously known as 'Mr. K'.
The engine was a smooth, twin-carbureted straight-six that sounded much like a Jaguar E-Type.
Unlike the earlier 240Z and 260Z, the 280Z used Bosch L-Jetronic fuel injection instead of carburetors.
To meet new US safety regulations, it featured larger, rubber-capped '5 mph' impact bumpers.
The 280Z is often considered the most reliable and easy-to-drive version of the original S30 Z-cars.
Nicknamed the 'Lil' Hustler' in American marketing campaigns.
It was the first Datsun truck to offer a 'King Cab' (extended cab) version.
The 620 featured a unique 'wingline' design on the side of the bed that made it instantly recognizable.
The Pantera combined exotic Italian styling with the reliability and easy maintenance of a Ford V8 engine.
Elvis Presley famously owned a yellow Pantera and once shot it with a handgun when it refused to start.
The GTS version featured flared wheel arches and a high-performance engine tuning.
The Diplomat was an export-only model that was never sold in the United States.
It was essentially a Plymouth body with a DeSoto grille and trim.
It was extremely popular as a taxi in South Africa and several South American countries.
The car's body is made of unpainted brushed 304 stainless steel.
Designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro and featured iconic gull-wing doors.
While it became a pop-culture icon in 'Back to the Future', the real car was notoriously slow and underpowered.
Built by Barreiros in Spain, it was the most prestigious and powerful car produced in Spain during the 70s.
It was the preferred car for high-ranking Spanish officials and government ministers.
Spanish Prime Minister Luis Carrero Blanco was in a Dodge 3700 GT when he was assassinated by ETA in 1973.
The original Challenger was built on the E-Body platform, shared with the Plymouth Barracuda.
The 2008 redesign was so faithful to the original 1970 model that it remained visually similar for its entire 15-year production run.
The Challenger was the last American muscle car to offer a full range of 'High Impact' colors like Plum Crazy and Go Mango.
The 'R/T' badge stands for 'Road/Track', signifying it as a dual-purpose performance vehicle.
In 1970, the R/T could be ordered with the 'Shaker' hood, where the air intake vibrated along with the engine.
The modern R/T is the entry-level V8 Challenger, known for its distinctive HEMI rumble.
The Scat Pack uses the '392' engine, a naturally aspirated V8 that produces 485 horsepower.
The logo is a 'Super Bee' wearing a helmet and a scarf, a throwback to Dodge's 1960s marketing.
It is often considered the 'sweet spot' of the Challenger range, offering near-Hellcat speed without the supercharger cost.
Features a literal hole in the hood with an intake scoop attached directly to the engine.
Because the scoop is attached to the engine block, it shakes and moves visibly as the engine revs.
It is one of the only production cars in the 21st century to feature a functional through-the-hood intake.
It features Brembo 4-piston brakes as standard to handle its nearly 500 horsepower.
The Scat Pack allows the driver to access 'Performance Pages' on the screen to see G-force and lap times.
It was designed as a direct competitor to the Ford Mustang GT with the Performance Pack.
Features massive fender flares that add 3.5 inches of width to the car.
The Widebody allows for much larger tires (305-section), which significantly improves lateral grip and handling.
It uses the front splitter and suspension tuning from the legendary Challenger Hellcat.
SRT is Dodge's dedicated performance division, similar to BMW's M or Mercedes' AMG.
The first modern Challenger SRT8 (2008) only came with a 5-speed automatic transmission.
SRT models include high-performance features like active exhaust and heavy-duty cooling systems.
The first production car capable of performing a wheelie (lifting the front tires) during a drag start.
It was so fast that the NHRA (National Hot Rod Association) officially banned it from competition without a roll cage.
It came with a 'Demon Crate' for $1 that included narrow front 'skinny' tires for the drag strip.
One of the air intakes is hidden inside the driver-side inner headlight (the 'Air-Catcher').
It comes with two keys: a Black key that limits the car to 500 hp, and a Red key that unlocks the full 700+ hp.
The supercharger alone requires about 80 horsepower just to be spun by the engine.
The SRT8 was the flagship model for the first several years of the Challenger's 21st-century comeback.
Early 6.1L models are becoming collector items as they represent the 'purest' modern Hemi engine.
It was replaced in 2015 by the Scat Pack and Hellcat models.
While it looks like a Challenger, the stock car version is a custom tube-frame chassis built for racing.
Dodge officially pulled out of NASCAR in 2012, but many 'Challenger' bodied stock cars are still used in lower series.
The stock car's body is made entirely of thin sheet metal or composite, with no functioning doors or windows.
The Widebody package was first introduced on the Hellcat to help it put its massive power to the ground.
It uses wider wheels and 305/35R20 Pirelli tires on all four corners.
The car is roughly 3.5 inches wider than a standard Challenger, giving it a much more aggressive stance.
The Charger has evolved from a 2-door fastback (Gen 1) to a muscle car (Gen 2) to a 4-door sedan (Modern).
The 1968–1970 generation is one of the most famous cars in cinema history, appearing in 'Bullitt' and 'The Fast and the Furious'.
In 2024, the Charger returned to its 2-door roots with an all-electric 'Daytona' version.
The 426 Hemi engine was so large it was nicknamed 'The Elephant'.
Only a small fraction of Chargers were built with the Hemi engine due to its extremely high cost and racing maintenance requirements.
It used a hemispherical combustion chamber design, which was originally developed for aircraft engines.
The Charger 500 was built specifically for NASCAR aerodynamics, featuring a flush-mounted rear window and a 1968 Coronet grille.
It was a failure on the track, leading to the creation of the more radical, winged Charger Daytona.
Only about 392 units were ever built, making it one of the rarest Chargers for collectors.
Classic R/T models came standard with heavy-duty suspension and brakes to match their high-output engines.
Modern R/T Chargers are the most common V8 sedans used by US law enforcement (the 'Pursuit' package).
The R/T badge was Dodge's premier performance mark before the 'SRT' name was introduced.
The Coronet was Dodge's primary full-size car before the Charger and Challenger existed.
The high-performance Coronet R/T was one of the fastest cars in America in the late 1960s.
By the early 70s, it had shifted to become a more family-oriented sedan and station wagon.
Confusingly, the '440' in the name was a trim level, not a reference to the 440 cubic inch engine (though you could order one with it).
It sat in the middle of the Coronet range, between the base model and the high-end 500.
It is a popular choice for drag racing builds because it shares the same platform as the Charger but is often cheaper to buy.
The Super Bee was Dodge's budget-priced muscle car, intended to compete with the Plymouth Road Runner.
The name is a play on the 'B-Body' platform on which it was built.
It featured a unique dashboard cluster from the Dodge Charger, which gave it a more high-end feel than the Plymouth competitor.

engine: Various HEMI V8s (e.g., 5.3L Super Red Ram)
funFacts: The Custom Royal was Dodge's flagship model during the 'Forward Look' design era.
The 1959 model featured extreme 'Tailfin' styling that was among the largest of any Chrysler product.
It was one of the first cars to offer the push-button 'PowerFlite' automatic transmission.

The 2013 reboot was based on an Alfa Romeo Giulietta platform, marking a rare Italian-American hybrid sedan.
In 1968, Dodge built a limited number of 'Hemi Darts' for professional drag racing only; they are now worth over $250,000.
The GT was the sporty, upscale trim of the Dart compact car.

The GT version was often paired with a vinyl roof, which was a major status symbol at the time.
The Swinger was the 2-door hardtop version of the Dart that became a muscle car legend in 'Swinger 340' trim.
Despite its small size, the Swinger 340 was capable of beating many big-block cars in a drag race due to its high-revving engine.
The 'Swinger' name was chosen to reflect the youthful, 'swinging' culture of the late 1960s.
The first-generation Durango was a mid-size SUV based on the Dodge Dakota pickup truck.
The Durango SRT Hellcat is one of the fastest 3-row SUVs in the world, with a 0-60 time of 3.5 seconds.
It is one of the few modern SUVs that can tow over 8,700 lbs, rivaling full-size trucks.
The 1974 Dodge Monaco is world-famous as the 'Bluesmobile' in the film 'The Blues Brothers'.
It was Dodge's flagship full-size car, competing with the Ford LTD and Chevrolet Caprice.
The 1974-1977 models were some of the largest passenger cars ever built, stretching over 18 feet in length.
Named after the Polaris star, fitting the 'Space Age' design trend of the early 1960s.
For a brief period in 1962, the Polara was dramatically downsized, which nearly ruined its sales until it was enlarged again.
It was extremely popular with state police forces due to its 'torsion-bar' suspension, which offered better stability than rivals.
The first-generation Viper had no external door handles, no side windows, and no permanent roof.
Its massive V10 engine was developed with help from Lamborghini, which Chrysler owned at the time.
It had no anti-lock brakes or traction control, earning it a reputation as one of the most dangerous and raw sports cars ever made.
The 'SWB' (Short Wheelbase) chassis was 200mm shorter than the standard 250 GT, making it much more agile.
It was the first Ferrari to be fitted with disc brakes as standard.
It is often cited by designers as one of the most perfectly proportioned cars ever built.
Passo Corto is the Italian name for 'Short Wheelbase'.
Only 176 examples were ever produced, with bodies built by Scaglietti.
It was highly successful in GT racing, winning its class at Le Mans three years in a row (1960-1962).
Widely considered the 'Holy Grail' of car collecting; one sold at auction for $70 million.
Enzo Ferrari personally vetted every original buyer of the car to ensure they were 'worthy'.
Only 36 were ever built, and every single one of them still exists today.
It was the first Ferrari to feature an all-aluminum space-frame chassis, which was 40% stiffer than the F355.

The design moved away from pop-up headlights to fixed, high-intensity discharge units for better aerodynamics.
The folding soft-top mechanism was so complex it required seven electric motors to operate.
Despite being a convertible, the glass engine cover was retained, though the engine is harder to see than in the coupe.
It was Ferrari's 20th road-going convertible model.
The 'i' in the name stands for 'Injection', as it was the first Ferrari grand tourer to use Bosch K-Jetronic fuel injection.
It was the first Ferrari to be offered with an automatic transmission (a GM-sourced 3-speed).

It features a super-fast automated manual gearbox that can shift in just 60 milliseconds.
It is 220 lbs lighter than the standard F430, achieved through extensive use of carbon fiber and the removal of sound deadening.
This is a non-road-legal racing version of the 458 Italia, designed for Ferrari's one-make racing series.

It was the first Challenge model to feature an F1-style dual-clutch transmission.

It has no stalks on the steering column; the turn signals, wipers, and high beams are all buttons on the steering wheel.
The small winglets in the front grille are flexible and deform at high speeds to reduce drag.
The 458 Speciale introduced 'Side Slip Angle Control' (SSC), a computer system that allows for controlled power-slides.
It was the last mid-engine Ferrari to feature a naturally aspirated V8 engine.
It features active aerodynamics, with flaps in the front and rear that open to balance downforce and drag.
It was the first car in the world to feature a mid-mounted engine and a folding hard-top roof.
