Despite the open top, Ferrari tuned the exhaust system so that the signature engine scream still fills the cabin.
The '488' in the name stands for the unitary displacement of each cylinder (488cc).
It marked Ferrari's return to turbocharging for its mid-engine V8 cars for the first time since the F40.
The engine features 'Variable Torque Management', which limits torque in lower gears to mimic the feel of a naturally aspirated engine.
The name 'Pista' is Italian for 'Track', highlighting its focus on high-performance driving.
It uses an 'S-Duct' in the front hood, an F1-inspired feature that channels air through the bumper and over the car for downforce.
The engine is the most powerful V8 in Ferrari history to that point, producing 710 horsepower.
The 488 Spider is only 110 lbs heavier than the GTB coupe, a very small penalty for a convertible.
The electric rear window can be lowered even with the roof up, acting as a 'wind stop' or a way to hear the engine better.
It can accelerate from 0 to 124 mph (200 km/h) in just 8.7 seconds.
The 'TR' stands for Testarossa, as this was the major evolution of the original 1980s icon.
The engine was lowered in the chassis compared to the original Testarossa to improve the car's center of gravity and handling.
It is one of the last Ferraris to use a Flat-12 engine configuration.
The engine is a slightly detuned version of the V12 found in the legendary Ferrari Enzo supercar.
It features 'flying buttresses' on the C-pillars that channel air to the rear of the car without the need for a spoiler.
Fiorano is the name of Ferrari's private test track where all their road and race cars are developed.
The most powerful naturally aspirated production car engine ever made at the time of its launch (789 hp).
The '812' name comes from 800 metric horsepower and 12 cylinders.
It was the first Ferrari to feature Electric Power Steering (EPS), used to integrate with the car's advanced stability controls.
The California was the first Ferrari to feature a front-mounted V8 engine.
It was designed as a more approachable, 'everyday' Ferrari for people who found other models too hardcore.
It was the first Ferrari to use a 7-speed dual-clutch transmission.
The 'T' stands for Turbo, marking a major shift in Ferrari's grand touring line toward more efficient power.
The interior features a 'Turbo Performance Engineer' (TPE) display between the vents that shows turbo pressure and efficiency.
Despite the turbos, Ferrari engineers worked extensively to ensure the engine still had a high-pitched, emotional sound.
It was the fastest road-legal Ferrari ever when it was released, excluding the limited-run hypercars.
Features an 'Aero Bridge' on the hood that directs air from the top of the car to the sides to increase downforce.
The engine produces a massive 730 horsepower and revs all the way to 8,700 RPM.
The 'tdf' stands for Tour de France, a legendary endurance race that Ferrari dominated in the 1950s and 60s.
Only 799 units were produced, and they are significantly lighter and more powerful than the standard F12.
It introduced 'Virtual Short Wheelbase', an active rear-wheel steering system that makes the long car feel much more agile.
The last car personally approved by Enzo Ferrari before his death.
It was the first production car to break the 200 mph (322 km/h) speed barrier.
The car was so focused on weight saving that the paint was so thin you could see the weave of the carbon-kevlar body underneath.
It introduced the 'Manettino' dial on the steering wheel, allowing the driver to change the car's character on the fly.
It was the first production car to use an E-Diff, a computer-controlled limited-slip differential derived from F1.
The design features two large air intakes at the front that are a direct tribute to the 1961 Ferrari 156 F1 car.
The engine was derived directly from the 3.5L V12 used in Ferrari's 1990 Formula 1 car.
Unlike the F40, the F50 used a naturally aspirated V12 and had a removable hardtop for open-air driving.
The engine is a stressed member of the chassis, meaning it is bolted directly to the carbon fiber tub with no subframe.
The engine is carried over from the 488 Pista, meaning the convertible gets 710 horsepower.
It features a 'Wall of Sound' exhaust that channels the engine note directly into the cabin.
The hard-top roof takes only 14 seconds to deploy or retract and can be operated at speeds up to 28 mph.
The name 'Tributo' is a tribute to the Ferrari V8 engine, which has won 'International Engine of the Year' many times.
It features a Lexan rear window with three louvers, a direct design reference to the legendary Ferrari F40.
It is 88 lbs lighter and 10% more aerodynamically efficient than the 488 GTB it replaced.
The name LaFerrari literally translates to 'The Ferrari' in Italian, intended to represent the definitive model.
It was the first Ferrari to use a hybrid powertrain, which it calls 'HY-KERS' (derived from Formula 1).
The seat is fixed in place to save weight; instead, the steering wheel and pedals move to meet the driver.
The 'Aperta' is the open-top version of the LaFerrari; only 210 units were ever produced.
Despite the missing roof, it has the exact same top speed (217 mph) and 0-60 time as the coupe.
The 210th car was built specifically to be auctioned for charity, where it sold for nearly $10 million.
Designed to evoke the carefree lifestyle of 1950s and 60s Rome, known as 'La Nuova Dolce Vita'.
It features a unique 5-position Manettino dial, rare for Ferrari's grand tourer models.
The rear spoiler is integrated into the rear screen and has three deployment positions depending on the car's speed.
Built to celebrate the 60th anniversary of the partnership between Ferrari and Pininfarina.
Only 6 units were produced, and Ferrari hand-picked the customers who were allowed to buy them.
It is based on the 458 Speciale but features completely custom bodywork with no windshield (though production versions had one).
It is Ferrari's first plug-in hybrid convertible, capable of driving about 15 miles on electric power alone.
With a combined 986 horsepower, it is one of the most powerful Ferraris ever made.
The front two electric motors allow for 'torque vectoring', pulling the car into corners with incredible precision.
The 'SF90' name celebrates the 90th anniversary of the Scuderia Ferrari racing team.
It is the first mid-engine Ferrari to feature all-wheel drive.
It lacks a traditional reverse gear in the transmission; instead, the front electric motors spin backward to move the car in reverse.
The name 'Testarossa' means 'Red Head' in Italian, referring to the red-painted valve covers on the engine.
The massive side 'cheese grater' slats were actually a safety requirement in some countries to prevent people from reaching into the radiators.
It became a worldwide cultural icon after being featured in the TV show 'Miami Vice'.
The 1100 was the backbone of Italian motoring in the 1950s, known for its reliability and robust design.
It was produced under license in India as the Premier Padmini until the year 2000.
Early models featured 'suicide doors' at the front (hinged at the rear).
The 'TV' stands for 'Turismo Veloce' (Fast Touring), which was the high-performance variant.
It featured a unique third fog light mounted right in the center of the grille.
It was a popular entry in the Mille Miglia race for amateur drivers who wanted a capable but affordable sports sedan.
Designed by Pininfarina, it is often called the 'mini Ferrari' because of its elegant, classic Italian lines.
It was one of the first mass-produced Italian convertibles designed for export to the American market.
The seats featured a unique 'swiveling' base to make it easier for ladies in skirts to get in and out of the low car.
The Fiat 124 won European Car of the Year in 1967.
It is one of the most produced car designs in history because Fiat sold the blueprints to Russia (Lada) and Turkey (Tofaş).
It featured advanced technology for a 1960s economy car, including four-wheel disc brakes and a synchronized gearbox.
Designed by Pininfarina and remained in production for nearly 20 years with very few styling changes.
The folding soft-top design was so simple and effective it could be operated with one hand from the driver's seat.
The high-performance Abarth version was a very successful rally car in the early 1970s.
The 126 was the successor to the original Fiat 500, using much of its mechanical layout in a more modern boxy body.
It was incredibly popular in Poland, where it was produced for 27 years and became a symbol of Polish independence.
The car's tiny dimensions made it easy to park in crowded European cities, often being pulled into spaces sideways.
The 'p' stands for Poland, where the car was built under license by FSM.
Its official nickname in Poland is 'Maluch', which means 'the small one' or 'toddler'.
By the time production ended in 2000, over 3.3 million units had been built in Poland alone.
The original 500 redefined the 'City Car' and motorized Italy after World War II.
The classic model's roof is canvas, not to make it a convertible, but to save on expensive steel during production.
The 2007 revival became an instant fashion icon and has been produced in hundreds of special editions, including one by Gucci.
The 'L' stands for Lusso (Luxury), which was the most popular and upscale trim of the classic 500.
It featured a horizontal 'modern' speedometer and a black steering wheel instead of the old white ivory-colored one.
It included an extra chrome bumper guard on the front and rear to protect the car in tight Italian parking spots.
The Fiat 600 was the first large-scale mass-produced car in Italy, preceding the smaller 500.
It featured 'suicide doors' (hinged at the rear) until 1964.
The Fiat 600 Multipla was the world's first true MPV (minivan), capable of carrying six people in a car only 11 feet long.
The 850 was developed as a more powerful and larger alternative to the Fiat 600.
The engine was designed to rotate counter-clockwise, a rare quirk in the automotive world.
It was available as a sedan, a beautiful coupe, and a spider (convertible) designed by Bertone.
Designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro during his time at the Bertone design house.
The headlights were angled back at a sharp degree to give it a more aerodynamic and sporty look than the sedan.
In America, it was a very popular entry-level sports car, often called the 'poor man's Ferrari'.
The Sporting model was the 'junior hot hatch' of the 1990s, famous for its bright Broom Yellow paint.
Despite only having 54 horsepower, its extremely low weight made it surprisingly fun and agile to drive.
It was one of the first cars to use the 'FIRE' (Fully Integrated Robotised Engine) produced on automated assembly lines.

regionalNames: RAM ProMaster (USA), Peugeot Boxer, Citroën Jumper
funFacts: The Ducato is the most popular base vehicle for motorhomes and RVs in Europe.
It was co-developed by Fiat and PSA (Peugeot/Citroën), which is why so many versions look identical.
Since 2013, it has been sold in North America as the RAM ProMaster.
The Uno was designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro and was an absolute masterclass in 'tall' packaging for a small car.
It used a single windshield wiper, a unique cost-saving and aerodynamic feature.
The Uno Turbo IE was a legendary 1980s hot hatch that could challenge much larger cars on the highway.
The final generation (105E) is famous for its 'backward-slanted' rear window that stayed clear in the rain.
It gained worldwide fame as the flying car (a turquoise 105E) in 'Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets'.
The Anglia was Ford of Britain's main entry-level car for nearly 30 years.
The first-generation Bronco was the first SUV developed specifically by Ford to compete with the Jeep CJ-5.
The 1994 Bronco gained notoriety during the low-speed chase of O.J. Simpson in Los Angeles.
The new 2021 Bronco allows owners to remove the doors and roof, and even store the doors inside the vehicle.
The original Consul was the first British Ford to feature a modern overhead-valve engine.
It was the mid-range model in Ford's 'Three Graces' lineup, sitting between the Anglia and the Zephyr.
In the 1970s, the Consul name was briefly brought back as a base-trim version of the Ford Granada.
The Corcel was actually based on the Renault 12, a result of Ford buying Willys-Overland do Brasil.
It was one of the most successful and important cars in the history of the Brazilian automotive market.
It was famous for its fuel efficiency and smooth ride compared to the more rugged VW Beetle competition.
The Mk3 is famous for its 'Coke Bottle' styling, a trend inspired by American muscle cars of the same era.
It was the car that established the Cortina as the dominant fleet and family car in the UK for a decade.
Known as the TC series (Taunus-Cortina) as Ford moved to harmonize their British and German model lines.
It was the last full-size rear-wheel-drive sedan in America built on a traditional heavy-duty body-on-frame chassis.
It was the near-exclusive choice for American police cars and taxis for 20 years due to its extreme durability.
The name 'Crown Victoria' originally referred to a chrome trim piece that arched over the roof of 1950s models.
The 1949 Ford Custom was the first all-new car design from the 'Big Three' after World War II.
It introduced 'ponton' styling, which integrated the fenders into the body for a smooth, slab-sided look.
The 1949 Custom was so successful it is credited with saving the Ford Motor Company from financial ruin.
The Customline was Ford's mid-tier model in the early 1950s, sitting above the base Mainline.
It was very popular in Australia, where it was produced in right-hand drive until 1959.
The 1954 model introduced the 'Y-Block' V8, which was Ford's first overhead-valve V8 engine.
The 1940 Deluxe is one of the most popular cars in the history of hot rodding and custom car culture.
It featured a high-end interior and extra chrome trim compared to the 'Standard' model.
The Flathead V8 engine in these cars was famous for its distinctive low rumble and easy power tuning.
The Ford Escape Hybrid (2004) was the first hybrid SUV to hit the global market.
It was co-developed with Mazda, who sold their version as the Mazda Tribute.
In 2020, the Escape moved to a much more car-like, rounded design to improve aerodynamics and fuel efficiency.
The Everest is a rugged SUV based on the chassis of the Ford Ranger pickup truck.
It is designed for global markets (Asia, Africa, Australia) where extreme off-road durability is required.
Unlike the Ranger it is based on, the Everest uses coil-spring rear suspension for a much more comfortable passenger ride.
The Explorer is credited with kickstarting the modern SUV craze in America during the early 1990s.
Early Explorers were based on the Ford Ranger truck, but modern versions use a dedicated unibody car platform.
The Explorer ST is a performance-tuned model with over 400 horsepower, designed by Ford Performance.
The F-1 was the first generation of the Ford F-Series, which has since become the best-selling vehicle in America for over 40 years.
It was marketed with the slogan 'Bonus Built' to emphasize its improved comfort and durability over pre-war trucks.
The F-1 was the half-ton version, while F-2 and F-3 were the heavy-duty models.
The F-100 replaced the F-1 in 1953 to celebrate Ford's 50th anniversary.
It is one of the most iconic shapes in American truck history, particularly the 'Step-side' bed models.
In 1984, the F-100 was officially replaced by the F-150 nameplate as the entry-level full-size truck.
The F-150 was originally introduced as a 'heavy half-ton' model to bypass new emissions regulations.
In 2015, Ford made the controversial move to build the entire F-150 body out of military-grade aluminum to save 700 lbs.
The F-150 Lightning (2022) is the first all-electric version and can even power a home during a blackout.
Named after Henry Ford’s estate, 'Fair Lane', in Dearborn, Michigan.
The 1957 Fairlane 500 Skyliner was the first mass-production car with a retractable hardtop roof.
The Fairlane Thunderbolt (1964) was a limited-edition drag racing car that is a legend in the muscle car world.
The Fairlane 500 was the top-of-the-line trim level for the Fairlane series.
In 1957, the Fairlane 500 helped Ford outsell Chevrolet for the first time since 1935.
The 1968-69 Fairlane 500 Fastbacks are highly desirable today for their aerodynamic 'Fastback' styling.
The Sunliner was the name specifically for the convertible version of the Ford Fairlane.
In 1955, it featured the iconic 'Fairlane Stripe'—a stainless steel trim piece that swept down the side of the car.
It was a status symbol of mid-century American prosperity, often seen in bright pastel colors.
The Fairmont was the first car built on Ford's legendary 'Fox Platform'.
Because it used the same chassis as the Mustang, many Fairmonts have been modified with high-performance racing parts.
It was designed as a lightweight, fuel-efficient replacement for the Ford Maverick during the fuel crisis.
The Futura was the upscale, sporty version of the compact Falcon, featuring bucket seats and a console.
The 1963 Falcon Futura Sprint served as the engineering testbed for the original 1964 1/2 Mustang.
Falcon Futuras were very successful in European rally racing, proving the durability of small American Fords.
The XP was the first Australian Falcon to win the 'Wheels Car of the Year' award.
Ford Australia held a 70,000-mile endurance trial at high speed to prove the XP was more durable than previous models.
It introduced the 'Fairmont' trim level to the Australian market as the top-tier luxury version.
The Focus was a revolution in the compact car world, featuring independent rear suspension that made it handle like a sports car.
The first generation's 'New Edge' design language was highly influential on early 2000s automotive styling.
It became the world's best-selling car nameplate in 2012, overtaking the Toyota Corolla.
The final generation (2016-2018) featured a famous 'Drift Mode' that sent power to the outside rear wheel to help the car slide.
Every Focus RS engine was hand-assembled by a small team at Ford's Saarlouis plant in Germany.
It used a unique 'GKN Twinster' rear drive unit instead of a traditional differential.
The second-generation Fusion (2013) gained fame for its 'Aston Martin-style' front grille.
It was one of the first mid-size sedans to offer a full range of powertrain options: gas, hybrid, and plug-in hybrid (Energi).
Ford officially discontinued the Fusion in 2020 as part of its shift away from sedans toward SUVs and trucks.
The name 'Galaxie' was inspired by the 1950s space race between the US and USSR.
The high-performance Galaxie 500XL was Ford's top-tier luxury-muscle hybrid.
The 1963 1/2 'Fastback' models were designed specifically to be more aerodynamic for NASCAR racing.
The 1972 model features a distinctive 'Fishmouth' grille that is a favorite among collectors.
The Gran Torino gained worldwide fame as the red and white 'Striped Tomato' in the TV show 'Starsky & Hutch'.
It was also the titular car in the 2008 Clint Eastwood film, 'Gran Torino'.
The European Granada was a high-end executive car, while the American version was a more compact luxury car.
American marketing famously compared the Ford Granada to the Mercedes-Benz 280, claiming they were almost indistinguishable in luxury.
The European version was a staple car for villains and police alike in 1970s British TV dramas like 'The Sweeney'.
The GXL was the absolute top-of-the-range model of the first-generation European Granada.
It came standard with a vinyl roof, sunroof, and real wood trim on the dashboard.
It was one of the first cars in Europe to offer a centralized 'check-panel' for light and fluid failures.
Created as a modern tribute to the GT40, the car that beat Ferrari at Le Mans in the 1960s.
The 2005 model is 3 inches taller than the original GT40, which is why it wasn't named the GT43.
The modern GT's flying buttresses are actually hollow and act as air intakes for the engine.
The Australian Landau was a luxury hardtop based on the Falcon XB but with hidden headlights from the LTD.
It was extremely exclusive, with only 1,385 units ever produced.
It featured four-wheel disc brakes, which was a very advanced feature for an Australian car in 1973.
The name 'LTD' was often thought to stand for 'Limited', but Ford executives once said it didn't officially stand for anything.
For much of the 70s, it was Ford's largest and most plush sedan, sitting above the Galaxie 500.
The 1980s 'LTD LX' was a rare performance version that used the 5.0L V8 and suspension from the Mustang GT.

layout: Front-Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive (Classic) / Front or AWD (Modern)
funFacts: The original Maverick was launched on April 17, 1969—exactly five years to the day after the original Mustang.
The name 'Maverick' was chosen to sound unbranded and independent, referencing the unbranded cattle in the Old West.
The modern Maverick pickup is so popular that Ford has had to stop taking orders multiple times to catch up with production.
The Model 18 was the first low-priced, mass-produced car to feature a V8 engine.
Known as the 'Deuce' due to the '2' in its year (1932), it is the most legendary car in hot rod history.
Clyde Barrow (of Bonnie and Clyde) famously wrote a letter to Henry Ford praising the 'dandy' V8 for being a great getaway car.
It was the long-awaited successor to the Model T and was the first Ford to use the standard set of driver controls (clutch, brake, throttle).
By 1929, Ford had already sold 2 million Model As.
It was the first car to feature a windshield made of safety glass.
The name 'Mondeo' comes from the Latin word 'mundus', meaning 'world', as it was intended to be a global car.
It introduced the driver-side airbag as standard across the entire range in 1993, a first for its class.
In the UK, the phrase 'Mondeo Man' was coined by politicians to describe the typical middle-class voter.
The Mustang created the 'Pony Car' segment—compact, affordable, sporty coupes.
Ford sold over 400,000 Mustangs in its first year, shattering all sales records.
The name was allegedly inspired by the P-51 Mustang fighter plane from WWII, not the horse.
Designed specifically to homologate the Mustang for the SCCA Trans-Am racing series.
The name 'Boss' came from the designer Larry Shinoda, who called his secret project 'the boss's car'.
It featured iconic 'hockey stick' stripes and a matte black hood to reduce glare for the driver.
The Bullitt edition is a tribute to the Highland Green Mustang driven by Steve McQueen in the 1968 film 'Bullitt'.
It is always stripped of spoilers, fog lights, and even the pony logo on the grille for a stealthy 'movie-car' look.
Each car features a unique chassis number on a plaque mounted to the dashboard.
The Dark Horse is the first new performance nameplate for the Mustang since the Bullitt in 2001.
It features a unique color-shifting 'Blue Ember' paint that changes depending on the light.
The wheels are made of lightweight carbon fiber, a feature usually reserved for ultra-expensive supercars.
Eleanor is a fictional custom Mustang GT500 created for the 2000 film 'Gone in 60 Seconds'.
The movie car was designed by Chip Foose and became so popular that it sparked a massive industry of recreations.
Eleven identical Eleanor cars were built for the movie, but only three were fully functional driving units.
The Fastback is widely considered the most beautiful and iconic of the classic Mustang body styles.
Early 1965 models featured functional air louvers on the C-pillar to vent the cabin.
The folding rear seat allowed for a long, flat cargo area, which Ford marketed as the '2+2' (seating for two plus two).
The 'GT' badge was first offered as a performance and appearance package in 1965.
The 1980s '5.0' Mustang GT became a symbol of the decade, featuring a high-output V8 and distinctive 'cheese grater' taillights.
The modern 5.0L Coyote V8 is capable of revving to 7,500 RPM, very high for an American pushrod-style displacement V8.
While often criticized by enthusiasts today, the Mustang II was a massive sales success, selling over 1.1 million units.
It was much smaller and more fuel-efficient than the 1973 model, designed to survive the Arab Oil Embargo.
The 'King Cobra' edition (1978) was a wild appearance package with a massive hood decal and T-Tops.
The name 'Mach 1' refers to the speed of sound.
The original 1969 model came with a 'Shaker' hood scoop that sat directly on the air cleaner through a hole in the hood.
In the modern 2021 version, it uses parts from the Shelby GT350 and GT500 to create the best-handling 5.0L Mustang ever.
The original 1965 GT350 was a raw racing car with no rear seats and a side-exit exhaust.
The modern version (2015-2020) features a 'Flat-Plane Crank' V8 that revs to 8,250 RPM and sounds like a European supercar.
Carroll Shelby famously said the name GT350 was chosen because his shop was 350 steps away from his office.
The modern GT500 is the most powerful street-legal Ford ever produced.
Its supercharger is so large it has to be cooled by its own independent water-cooling circuit.
In the 1960s, the GT500 featured a larger 'Big Block' V8 than the GT350, making it the ultimate high-speed cruiser.
The original Puma was a small coupe based on the Fiesta, famous for its high-revving engine developed by Yamaha.
The Puma name was revived in 2019 for a compact crossover SUV.
The modern Puma features a 'MegaBox'—a deep, waterproof storage well in the trunk with a drain plug for easy cleaning.
The Ranchero was the first 'Coupe Utility' vehicle in America, beating the Chevrolet El Camino to market by two years.
Over its life, it was based on five different platforms, ranging from the full-size Ford to the compact Falcon.
Ford marketing claimed it was 'More than a car! More than a truck!'.
The Ranger is sold in almost every country in the world, though the US version was discontinued for several years before returning in 2019.
The 'Ranger Raptor' is a desert-racing variant with a reinforced frame and high-performance Fox bypass shocks.
Early Rangers were small compact trucks, but modern versions have grown into mid-size territory.
It was the first 'sporty' minivan (MPV) that didn't compromise on handling, winning European Car of the Year in 2007.
Top-tier versions used the same 5-cylinder engine as the Ford Focus ST and the Volvo V70.
It pioneered a 'fold-flat' seating system where all five rear seats could be stowed into the floor with one pull.
The Taunus was Ford's primary family car in Germany for over 40 years.
The 1960s 'M-Series' Taunus used a front-wheel-drive layout, which was very unusual for a Ford of that era.
The name comes from the Taunus mountain range near Ford's factory in Cologne, Germany.
The Thunderbird was originally created as a response to the Chevrolet Corvette, but it focused more on luxury than racing.
The 1955-1957 models featured iconic 'porthole' windows in the removable hardtop.
It was the first production car to use sequential rear turn signals, where the three lights blink in a row.
The Transit is the best-selling light commercial vehicle in Europe and is often called 'The Backbone of Britain'.
Scotland Yard once called the Transit 'Britain's most wanted van' because it was used in 95% of bank robberies in the 1970s.
Ford has built several 'Supervans' over the years, including one powered by a Formula 1 V8 engine.
The ZIM was the first Soviet luxury car designed to be used by high-ranking officials below the rank of top leaders.
It was the first car in the USSR to feature a fluid coupling for a smooth semi-automatic-like driving experience.
Despite its large size and luxury status, it used a unibody construction, which was very advanced for the early 1950s.
The name 'Chaika' means 'The Seagull' in Russian.
It was exclusively produced for high-ranking members of the Soviet government and foreign diplomats.
Its design was heavily influenced by American luxury cars of the mid-50s, particularly Packard and Cadillac.
The Volga was the most prestigious car available for purchase by ordinary citizens in the Soviet Union.
It featured an iconic 'leaping deer' ornament on the hood (which was removed in later series for pedestrian safety).
Known for its immense durability and high ground clearance, it was perfectly suited for the rough Soviet roads.
The GAZ-24 was the workhorse of the Soviet Union, used as taxis, police cars, and ambulances for decades.
The KGB version (GAZ-24-24) featured a massive V12 engine from the Chaika hidden under the hood of a standard-looking sedan.
Its simple, boxy design remained largely unchanged for over 15 years.
The GV70 features a fingerprint authentication system that allows the driver to start the car without a key.
Its interior design is inspired by the aerodynamic shape of an aircraft wing, which Genesis calls the 'Beauty of White Space'.
It was named MotorTrend’s SUV of the Year in 2022, shortly after its global launch.
Based on the 1956 British Morris Oxford Series III, it remained in production for an incredible 56 years with minimal changes.
For decades, it was the only car used by the Indian Prime Minister and high-ranking government officials.
In 2013, Top Gear named the Ambassador the 'World's Best Taxi' for its durability and interior space.
Holden was Australia's own car brand before General Motors shut it down in 2020.
The Colorado was developed in collaboration with Isuzu and was one of the most popular work vehicles in Australia and New Zealand.
The high-end 'Z71' trim was designed to compete with the Ford Ranger Wildtrak in the 'lifestyle truck' segment.

engine: Various options including Turbo I4 and Hybrid
funFacts: The Accord was the first car from a Japanese manufacturer to be produced in the United States (1982).
It has been on the Car and Driver '10 Best' list a record-shattering 38 times.
The early 1990s Accord wagons are famous for being incredibly durable, with many examples surpassing 500,000 miles.
The Amaze was Honda's first diesel car offered in India, developed specifically for emerging markets.
It is a 'sub-4-meter' sedan, designed to take advantage of lower tax brackets in India for small vehicles.
Despite its tiny exterior dimensions, it uses Honda's 'Man Maximum, Machine Minimum' philosophy to offer a surprisingly large cabin.
The Civic famously helped Honda survive the 1973 oil crisis due to its incredible fuel efficiency.
It introduced the 'CVCC' engine, which was so clean it met US emissions standards without needing a catalytic converter.
The Civic has evolved from a subcompact hatchback into one of the world's most popular mid-size family cars.
The FD2 was the only Type R model based on the 4-door sedan body style rather than the hatchback.
It featured a much stiffer chassis and more power than the European-market FN2 Civic Type R.
It used a unique 'Championship White' paint, a signature color for all legendary Honda Type R models.
The 'Si' stands for 'Sport Injected', as it was one of the first Hondas to feature electronic fuel injection.
For decades, the Si was the highest-performance Civic available in North America before the Type R arrived.
The 2006-2011 generation Si featured an 8,000 RPM redline, giving it a true 'race car' feel.
The SiR was the first production car to feature Honda's legendary VTEC (Variable Valve Timing and Lift Electronic Control) system.
The B16A engine was the first naturally aspirated production engine to exceed 100 horsepower per liter.
In Japan, the SiR was the top-spec Civic before the 'Type R' nameplate was officially launched.
The original EK9 Type R featured a hand-ported engine head, just like a professional racing engine.
It consistently holds the record for the fastest front-wheel-drive car around the Nürburgring Nordschleife.
Every Type R features a red Honda badge, a tribute to Honda's first Formula 1 win.
The FL5 generation is the most powerful Civic ever sold in the United States.
Despite its massive rear wing, the car's design is much more subtle and 'grown-up' than the previous 'Gundam-style' FK8 generation.
It features a dedicated 'LogR' data logger that displays tire friction and lap times on the infotainment screen.
The original CR-V came with a folding picnic table hidden under the rear cargo floor.
It was one of the first SUVs to be based on a car chassis (the Civic) rather than a truck frame.
Early models featured a spare tire mounted on the rear tail-gate, a design trait that was later removed for a cleaner look.
The CR-X HF (High Fuel) was so efficient that it could achieve over 50 miles per gallon in the 1980s.
It was famous for its 'vertical' rear glass window, which provided better visibility than traditional hatchbacks.
Enthusiasts call it a 'Pocket Rocket' due to its tiny size and go-kart-like handling.
It was marketed as the spiritual successor to the CR-X, but with a modern hybrid powertrain.
It was the first hybrid car in the world to be offered with a 6-speed manual transmission.
It featured a 'Sport' button that adjusted the electric motor's assist to provide a temporary boost of power.
The car was so short that in Japan, it was marketed as a '2-seater' to avoid certain taxes.
The 1988-1991 Si models are highly coveted for their perfect balance and minimalist interior.
It uses a torsion bar front suspension, which was unique compared to the MacPherson struts of its rivals.
Widely regarded as the best-handling front-wheel-drive car of all time.
The chassis was manually seam-welded to increase rigidity, and the windshield glass was 10% thinner to save weight.
The Japanese-market models (DC2) had long, rectangular headlights, while the US models had four round headlights.
The second-generation NSX uses three electric motors: two on the front wheels and one on the rear.
It features a 'Quiet Mode' that allows the car to drive at low speeds using only electric power.
The first generation was the world's first production car to feature an all-aluminum body.
The Prelude was Honda's technological showcase, often debuting features like 4-wheel steering and an early navigation system.
The 1988 model's 4-wheel steering system was completely mechanical, while later versions used computers.
It was the first Honda model to offer a power moonroof as standard equipment.
The S2000's engine (F20C) held the record for the highest specific power for a naturally aspirated engine for over a decade (120 hp/liter).
The engine was mounted entirely behind the front axle, giving the car a perfect 50:50 weight distribution.
The digital instrument cluster was inspired by the displays used in Formula 1 racing cars of the 90s.
The 'AP2' update increased engine displacement to 2.2L to provide more low-end torque for the American market.
The redline was lowered from 9,000 RPM to 8,000 RPM to accommodate the longer piston stroke.
It introduced carbon fiber synchros in the transmission for smoother, more durable shifting.
The 'Blue' designation in some markets referred to an ultra-efficient version with low-rolling-resistance tires.
It is one of the world's most popular subcompact cars, sold under different names in over 100 countries.
In many emerging markets, it was the first car to offer a 5-year/100,000-mile warranty as standard.
The Elantra is Hyundai's best-selling model ever, with over 13 million units sold globally.
The 2021 model (CN7) features a very radical 'Parametric Dynamics' design with sharp triangle-shaped creases in the doors.
The high-performance Elantra N uses technology directly from Hyundai's WRC rally program.
It features an 'e-Shift' mode that simulates the feel and sound of an 8-speed dual-clutch transmission.
It has a 'N Drift Optimizer' that helps manage the torque to maintain a perfect drift angle.
It is the most powerful production Hyundai ever made, reaching 60 mph in just 3.2 seconds.
The Palisade is Hyundai's largest SUV, designed specifically for the North American family market.
It features an 'Intercom' system that allows the driver to talk to passengers in the third row through the speakers.
The design of its daytime running lights (DLRs) is meant to mimic the look of a crocodile's eye peaking out of the water.
Named after the city of Tucson, Arizona.
The fourth-generation Tucson (2020) features 'Hidden Lights' that appear to be part of the chrome grille when turned off.
It is currently Hyundai's best-selling SUV globally.
The Paykan was based on the British Hillman Hunter and became the national car of Iran.
The name 'Paykan' means 'Arrow' in Persian.
It was so successful that it motorized the entire country, remaining the primary mode of transport for millions of Iranians for four decades.
Imperial was Chrysler's prestige brand, and the LeBaron was its top-tier trim level.
The 1957 model was the first production car to offer 'Cruise Control' (which they called 'Auto-Pilot').
Its chassis was so over-engineered and strong that it was eventually banned from demolition derbies because it was 'unfair' to other cars.
The 'Southampton' name designated the pillarless hardtop body style.
It featured 'FliteSweep' deck lids with a simulated spare tire mold on the trunk, a highly polarizing style choice of the era.
It used a push-button automatic transmission located to the left of the steering wheel.
The QX60 is Infiniti's best-selling 3-row luxury crossover.
It features a unique 'one-touch' second-row seat that can slide forward even with a child safety seat installed.
The 2022 redesign moved from a CVT transmission to a more responsive 9-speed automatic from ZF.
Enzo Ferrari famously called the E-Type 'the most beautiful car ever made'.
It was surprisingly affordable when launched, offering 150 mph performance for a fraction of the cost of a Ferrari.
The car's iconic 'clamshell' hood is one of the largest single pieces of automotive sheet metal ever produced.
It was Jaguar's first-ever SUV in the brand's 94-year history.
It won both 'World Car of the Year' and 'World Car Design of the Year' in 2017.
The chassis uses 80% aluminum to save weight, a higher percentage than almost any other vehicle in its class.
The F-Type was marketed as the spiritual successor to the legendary E-Type.
Its active exhaust system is famous for its aggressive 'pops and crackles' on overrun.
The structure is made using cold-formed aluminum, which is joined with rivets and aerospace adhesive rather than welding.
Waymo (Google's self-driving division) chose the I-PACE as its primary vehicle for its all-electric autonomous taxi fleet.
The Waymo version features a massive roof-mounted LiDAR sensor that can 'see' 360 degrees for up to 300 meters.
It is the first-ever premium electric vehicle from a traditional European luxury brand to challenge Tesla.
The Mark 2 was the original 'sport sedan', capable of carrying four adults at speeds over 120 mph.
It was the favorite getaway car for British bank robbers in the 1960s, as it was faster than most police cars.
It is famous for its role in the TV series 'Inspector Morse', where the lead character drives a burgundy Mark 2.
It was Jaguar's first-ever compact executive car, designed to compete with the BMW 3 Series.
It was famously based on the platform of the Ford Mondeo, which led to criticism from some Jaguar purists.
It was the first Jaguar to offer a station wagon (Estate) body style as a production model.
The XF marked a radical departure from Jaguar's traditional retro styling to a modern, sleek design led by Ian Callum.
Upon starting the car, the circular gear selector rises out of the center console and the air vents rotate open.
The first generation (X250) was the first Jaguar to be designed entirely using computer-aided software (CAD).
The Series 3 design was subtley updated by the legendary Italian house Pininfarina.
It is famous for having two separate fuel tanks with two individual fuel filler caps (one on each rear fender).
Despite its age, many collectors consider the Series 3 XJ to be the most comfortable-riding car ever built.
It replaced the legendary E-Type, which made its wedge-shaped design very controversial at the time of launch.
It features iconic 'flying buttresses' on the C-pillars that create a unique and aerodynamic rear profile.
The XJS was in production for 21 years, one of the longest lifespans for any luxury grand tourer.
The 1996 XK8 was the first Jaguar to use their newly developed AJ-V8 engine.
The second generation (X150) was designed by Ian Callum and was built using an all-aluminum monocoque chassis.
It was famously inspired by the design of the original E-Type but updated for the modern era.
The '120' in the name referred to the car's 120 mph top speed, making it the fastest production car in the world at the time.
The first 242 cars were built with aluminum bodies over an ash wood frame before Jaguar switched to steel to meet high demand.
Clark Gable was one of the first owners, famously falling in love with the car's flowing lines.
The original Cherokee (SJ) was the first vehicle to use the term 'Sport Utility Vehicle' (SUV) in its marketing brochure.
The 1984 Cherokee (XJ) is considered the first 'modern' SUV because it used a unibody design rather than a truck frame.
The XJ Cherokee was so popular that it remained in production for 18 years almost unchanged.
The first-generation Compass was Jeep's first attempt at a front-wheel-drive crossover for 'city dwellers'.
It was the first Jeep to use a continuously variable transmission (CVT).
The Trailhawk trim is 'Trail Rated', meaning it can handle legitimate off-road conditions that would stop other compact crossovers.
The Trailhawk is the most off-road capable version of the Grand Cherokee luxury SUV.
It features the 'Quadra-Drive II' system, which includes a rear electronic limited-slip differential (ELSD).
It comes with unique red tow hooks on the front bumper that can withstand double the car's weight in pulling force.
The Wrangler is the direct descendant of the World War II 'Willys MB' military Jeep.
It is one of the only modern vehicles where the owner can still legally fold the windshield down flat against the hood.
The first generation (YJ) was famous for its square headlights, which many purists hated at the time.
The Forte was designed by Peter Schreyer, the man responsible for the original Audi TT.
The Forte GT-Line features a 10-year/100,000-mile powertrain warranty, one of the longest in the industry.
It replaced the aging Kia Spectra and transformed Kia's reputation in the compact car segment.
At various times, the Rio was the least expensive new car for sale in the United States.
It has been a top choice for 'B-Segment' rally racing in many parts of the world due to its simplicity.
Despite its small size, modern Rios scored a Top Safety Pick award from the IIHS.
The Soluto was developed primarily for the Chinese market but has become a hit in the Philippines and Vietnam.
It is positioned below the Rio as an ultra-affordable, basic family sedan.
It features a surprising amount of modern tech for its price, including a 7-inch touchscreen with Apple CarPlay.
The first-generation Sportage was based on a Mazda Bongo van platform.
It was one of the first Korean SUVs to achieve significant sales success in Europe and America.
The 2023 model features a massive 'curved' panoramic display that covers half of the dashboard.
Based on the Fiat 124, but redesigned with over 800 modifications to handle the harsh Russian winter.
The metal used in the body was thicker than the original Fiat, making it significantly heavier but more durable.
It motorized the Soviet Union and is still one of the most common sights on roads in many former Eastern Block countries.
Commonly known as the 'Riva' in the UK, it was one of the cheapest new cars available in the West during the 80s.
It remained in production for 30 years with almost no changes to its boxy, angular design.
The 2105 (and its sibling, the 2107) are often called 'The Russian Brick' for their blocky shape.
The Granta was developed in collaboration with Renault and was the best-selling car in Russia for several years.
It was designed to be the world's cheapest modern car, starting at roughly $7,000 USD upon launch.
It features a unique 'raised' suspension as standard to cope with the poorly maintained roads in rural Russia.
The Niva was the world's first mass-produced off-road vehicle to use a unibody construction (no truck frame).
It has been in continuous production for over 45 years with only minor mechanical updates.
A Niva was used by the Soviet Antarctic Expedition for 10 years, where it operated in temperatures as low as -54°C.
Named after a legendary Spanish fighting bull that fought in Zaragoza in 1993.
It was the first production Lamborghini to use a carbon fiber monocoque tub, which weighs only 324 lbs.
The suspension was inspired by Formula 1 cars, using a 'pushrod' design with horizontally mounted dampers.
The 'J' stands for Jota, referencing the Appendix J racing regulations and the legendary Miura Jota.
It is a true 'barchetta' with no roof, no windshield, and no air conditioning to save weight.
Only one was ever produced and was sold to a private collector for $2.8 million immediately after its debut.
The SV (Superveloce) is 110 lbs lighter than the standard Aventador thanks to a massive fixed rear wing and carbon fiber trim.
It was the first production Lamborghini to break the 7-minute barrier at the Nürburgring (6:59.73).
The interior is stripped out, with the infotainment system being a no-cost option to save weight.
Features 'Magnetic Ride Control' suspension that can change its stiffness in just a few milliseconds.
The 0-60 mph sprint takes only 2.8 seconds, making it one of the fastest-accelerating cars of its time.
Its massive rear wing is manually adjustable to three different positions to change the car's balance for different tracks.
The SVJ features 'ALA 2.0' (Aerodinamica Lamborghini Attiva), an active system that opens and closes flaps to stall the wing for higher top speeds.
It held the lap record for production cars at the Nürburgring with a time of 6:44.97.
Only 900 units were produced, plus an additional 63 'SVJ 63' editions to celebrate Lamborghini's founding year, 1963.
The word 'Countach!' is an exclamation of astonishment in the Piedmontese dialect, often used when seeing something beautiful.
It pioneered the iconic 'scissor doors' that have become a trademark for all flagship Lamborghinis since.
Visibility was so poor that drivers often had to sit on the door sill and look backward over the roof while reversing.
The Diablo GT was a track-oriented version that was only sold in Europe.
It featured a massive air intake scoop on the roof that fed cool air directly into the engine.
Only 80 units were ever built, making it one of the rarest and most desirable Diablo variants.
The 'VT' stands for Viscous Traction, Lamborghini's early all-wheel-drive system.
This was the final version of the Diablo, updated by Audi designers to be more modern and refined.
It used headlights from the Nissan 300ZX, as Lamborghini couldn't afford to develop its own for such a small production run.
The Gallardo is the best-selling Lamborghini model in history, with over 14,000 units produced.
It was designed as a 'daily driver' supercar, with better visibility and a more reliable Audi-developed electrical system.
The Italian State Police were given several Gallardos to use as high-speed interceptors and for emergency organ transport.
The '560' refers to the horsepower (PS), and the '4' refers to its all-wheel-drive system.
It introduced the 5.2L 'Odd-Fire' V10, which became the base for the future Huracán and Audi R8 engines.
It featured a new LED 'Y-shape' daytime running light design that became a standard look for the brand.
The engine was significantly more fuel-efficient and emitted less CO2 than the original 5.0L V10.
It featured a revised 'E-Gear' automated transmission that offered 40% faster shift times.
The front bumper design was inspired by the ultra-exclusive Lamborghini Reventón.
This was the convertible version of the track-focused Gallardo Superleggera.
The 'Performante' name was used to signify that it was the highest-performing open-top model.
It is 143 lbs lighter than the standard Gallardo Spyder, using carbon fiber for the engine cover and rear diffuser.
The name 'Superleggera' is Italian for 'Super Light'.
To save weight, the interior door panels were made of carbon fiber and the side windows were replaced with polycarbonate.
The 2010 version could accelerate from 0 to 124 mph in just 10.2 seconds.
The Huracán was the first production car to use 'LPI' (Lamborghini Piattaforma Inerziale), a system of sensors that measures the car's movements in 3D.
It features a naturally aspirated V10 engine, making it one of the last supercars to avoid turbochargers.
The digital dashboard can be fully customized, allowing the driver to show a massive navigation map or a traditional tachometer.
The EVO introduced rear-wheel steering and a torque-vectoring system that works on all four wheels simultaneously.
It features a 10.1-inch vertical touchscreen in the center console, which was a first for a Lamborghini supercar.
The exhaust tips are positioned high up in the rear bumper, similar to the Huracán Performante race car.
It introduced 'Forged Composites', a new type of carbon fiber made from chopped strands that gives a unique marble-like appearance.
It famously broke the Nürburgring production car record in 2016 with a time of 6:52.01.
It uses active aerodynamics (ALA) that can stall the wing on one side while keeping downforce on the other to help the car steer through corners.
It is the fastest and most powerful convertible in the Huracán family.
Despite the folding roof, it retains the advanced active aerodynamic (ALA) system found on the coupe.
The convertible top can be opened in 17 seconds and features a unique 'fin' design that channels air away from the cabin.
The soft-top roof is available in three colors: Black, Brown, and Red.
It features a power rear window that can be lowered even with the roof up, allowing the V10 engine sound to enter the cabin directly.
The chassis is a hybrid of aluminum and carbon fiber, providing the stiffness of a coupe with the freedom of a convertible.
STO stands for 'Super Trofeo Omologata', meaning it is a road-legal version of the Huracán Super Trofeo race car.
Over 75% of the body panels are made of carbon fiber to reduce weight to just 2,952 lbs.
It features a 'Cofango', a unique one-piece hood and fender assembly that flips forward, inspired by the Lamborghini Miura.
The Tecnica is designed as the 'perfect compromise' between the standard Evo and the extreme, track-only STO.
It features a unique 'Y' shape in the front bumper that is a tribute to the Lamborghini Terzo Millennio concept car.
The rear window is vertical, a change from other Huracáns that improves visibility and aerodynamic efficiency.
The Revuelto is Lamborghini's first series-production plug-in hybrid (PHEV) supercar.
Its V12 engine is the lightest and most powerful V12 Lamborghini has ever produced, even before the electric motors are added.
The car features 13 different driving modes, including a 'Città' (City) mode that is entirely electric.
It was the first car in the world to use a supercapacitor for hybridization, which is three times more powerful than a battery of the same weight.
The 'FKP 37' in the name honors the late Ferdinand Karl Piëch, born in 1937, who was instrumental in Audi's acquisition of Lamborghini.
Only 63 coupes and 19 roadsters were produced, all of which were sold before the car was even revealed.
The Temerario is the successor to the Huracán and marks the end of the naturally aspirated V10 era for Lamborghini.
Its V8 engine can rev to a massive 10,000 RPM, a world-first for a production twin-turbocharged supercar.
Like the Revuelto, it can drive in pure electric mode for short distances.
The Urus is the world's first 'Super SUV', combining supercar performance with the practicality of an SUV.
It features the largest carbon-ceramic brakes ever fitted to a production car (440mm in the front).
Despite its size, it can accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in just 3.1 seconds (Performante trim).
The Lancia Delta is the most successful rally car of all time, winning 6 consecutive World Rally Championship titles (1987-1992).
The 'Evoluzione' models featured adjustable rear spoilers and wide, aggressive fender flares.
It was one of the first cars to use a Torsen-based center differential to split power between the front and rear wheels.
The '110' refers to the length of the wheelbase in inches (on the classic models).
The new 2020 Defender uses an all-aluminum monocoque that is three times stiffer than a traditional body-on-frame design.
The classic Defender remained essentially unchanged for 33 years and is a global icon of off-road adventure.
The first-generation Range Rover Sport was actually based on the chassis of the Land Rover LR3 (Discovery 3).
It features a 'Wade Sensing' system that uses sonar to tell the driver how deep the water is when crossing rivers.
The SVR version was the first Land Rover to wear the 'SVR' performance badge.
The Series III was the first Land Rover to feature a modern plastic dashboard and the headlights moved to the outer fenders.
It is the most produced Land Rover Series model, with over 440,000 units built.
It was famous for having 'tropical' roof vents that allowed air to circulate in hot climates.
LaSalle was created by General Motors as a 'companion' brand for Cadillac, sitting just below it in price.
It was the first American car brand to be designed by a professional stylist (Harley Earl).
The Model 328 was known for its elegant, slender grille which was a major departure from the boxy look of the 20s.
The first-generation IS introduced the 'Altezza-style' clear taillights that became a massive customization trend in the 2000s.
It was designed as a direct challenger to the BMW 3 Series, with a focus on youthful, sporty driving.
The IS-F (2007) was the first Lexus model to feature the 'F' high-performance badge.
The IS200 is unique because it used a smooth straight-six engine instead of a 4-cylinder found in many rivals.
Its dashboard featured a famous 'Chronograph' instrument cluster that looked like a high-end Swiss watch.
In Japan, this model was sold as the Toyota Altezza and was a favorite for professional drift racing.
The car that launched the Lexus brand and forced Mercedes and BMW to completely rethink their luxury car engineering.
It was developed by 1,400 engineers and 2,300 technicians over six years at a cost of over $1 billion.
A famous commercial showed a pyramid of champagne glasses stacked on the engine hood while the car was 'running' at 145 mph on a dyno.
The RX is widely credited with inventing the 'Luxury Crossover' segment.
The RX 400h (2005) was the world's first luxury hybrid vehicle.
It is consistently the best-selling model in the entire Lexus lineup.
The design of the SC was sculpted using clay in California to give it a more organic, fluid look than Japanese rivals.
The SC300 shared its 2JZ engine with the Toyota Supra, making it a popular choice for high-horsepower modifications.
It was the first production car to feature a complex 'four-link' door hinge that allowed the door to move out and forward.
The 1961 Continental is famous for its 'suicide' (rear-hinged) doors and its extremely clean, understated design.
It was the first American car to offer a 2-year/24,000-mile warranty in 1961.
Presidents from Kennedy to Reagan used armored Lincoln Continentals as their primary state vehicles.
The Mark IV introduced the 'Opera Window'—a small, oval-shaped window in the C-pillar that became a huge trend.
Lincoln offered 'Designer Series' versions of the Mark IV, with interiors styled by Cartier, Givenchy, Bill Blass, and Pucci.
It featured a massive radiator-style grille that was meant to evoke the luxury of a Rolls-Royce.
The Mark V is one of the longest two-door coupes ever produced, measuring over 19 feet long.
Despite being smaller than the Mark IV, it used a more efficient design and was actually 400 lbs lighter.
It featured functional 'Louvered' air vents on the front fenders, which helped cool the massive V8 engine.
The MKS was the first Lincoln to feature the 'split-wing' grille, which was inspired by the 1941 Lincoln Zephyr.
It was the first luxury sedan to offer Ford's 'MyLincoln Touch' infotainment system.
The EcoBoost version offered V8-like power with the fuel economy of a V6, a major marketing point at the time.
Ford provided the bodies, but Lotus performed all the mechanical work to turn the humble Cortina into a race winner.
It was famous for its white paint with a single green stripe down the side.
Legendary racer Jim Clark was so successful in the Lotus Cortina that he won the 1964 British Saloon Car Championship.
The Elise used a revolutionary bonded aluminum chassis that was extremely light and rigid.
Named after Elisa Artioli, the granddaughter of Romano Artioli, the chairman of Lotus at the time.
The Elise had no power steering and very few driver aids, prioritizing a pure 'man-machine' connection.
The Emira is the very last internal combustion engine car that Lotus will ever produce.
It features an AMG-sourced 4-cylinder engine, marking a high-performance partnership between Lotus and Mercedes.
Its aerodynamic design produces significant downforce without the need for an active rear spoiler.
The Maruti 800 was the car that motorized India, similar to the VW Beetle in Germany or the Fiat 500 in Italy.
For many years, it was the best-selling car in India, with over 2.8 million units produced.
It was so small and reliable that it was often used to transport families of five plus their luggage.
The original Ghibli was designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro and is considered one of the best-looking grand tourers ever.
The name 'Ghibli' refers to a hot, dry wind in the Libyan desert.
The modern Ghibli was Maserati's first-ever production car to be offered with a diesel engine.

layout: Front-Mid Engine, Rear or All-Wheel Drive
funFacts: The first-generation GranTurismo used a V8 engine built by Ferrari, giving it one of the best exhaust notes in the world.
It was designed by Pininfarina and holds one of the longest production runs for a modern GT car (12 years for the first gen).
The 2024 'Folgore' version is Maserati's first-ever fully electric car.
The MC12 was based on the Ferrari Enzo but was longer, wider, and slightly slower on top speed due to higher downforce.
It was built specifically to homologate a racing version for the FIA GT Championship.
Only 50 road cars were ever produced, all in a signature White and Blue color scheme.
It features Maserati's first in-house developed engine in over 20 years, called the 'Nettuno'.
The engine uses a 'Twin Combustion' system derived from Formula 1 cars.
The entire car was developed in just 24 months using Maserati's advanced Virtual Vehicle Dynamics Development simulator.
This is a non-road-legal racing version built for the Fanatec GT2 European Series.
It features a massive adjustable rear wing and a roof scoop to help the Nettuno engine breathe under racing stress.
It marks Maserati's return to GT racing after the legendary MC12 era.

layout: Front-Mid Engine, Rear or All-Wheel Drive
funFacts: The name 'Quattroporte' simply means 'four doors' in Italian.
The first generation was the fastest 4-door sedan in the world when it launched in 1963.
It has been the official state car for the President of the Italian Republic for decades.
The modern Mazda3 is widely praised for having an interior that rivals luxury brands like Audi and Mercedes.
The 2019 model introduced 'Skyactiv-X', the world's first commercial gasoline engine to use compression ignition like a diesel.
The hatchback version features a very bold 'C-pillar' design that makes the rear half of the car look like a solid piece of sculpture.
The CX-5 was the first Mazda to feature the 'Kodo' design language (Soul of Motion).
It was also the first model to fully implement 'Skyactiv' technology across the engine, transmission, and chassis.
It is consistently praised for being the best-handling vehicle in its class, often called the 'SUV for people who love to drive'.
The MX-5 is the best-selling two-seater sports car in history, with over 1 million units sold.
The original design was intended to capture the spirit of 1960s British sports cars like the Lotus Elan, but with Japanese reliability.
The current ND generation is actually shorter and lighter than the generation it replaced, a rare feat in modern car design.
The name 'Miata' comes from an Old High German word for 'Reward'.
Enthusiasts use the acronym 'M.I.A.T.A.'—Miata Is Always The Answer.
The first-generation 'NA' models are famous for their round pop-up headlights that look like eyes.
The RX-7 is the only mass-produced sports car to use a twin-rotor Wankel engine.
Its compact rotary engine allowed for a very low center of gravity and a 50:50 weight balance.
The car's logo changed several times, but always symbolized the triangular shape of the rotary engine's piston.
The 'FB' was the first generation of the RX-7 and was designed to replace the RX-3.
It was an instant racing success, winning its class at the 24 Hours of Daytona in its very first attempt in 1979.
The engine was so small it was mounted completely behind the front axle, technically making it a 'front-mid-engine' car.
The FD is famous for its complex sequential twin-turbo system, where one turbo provides boost at low RPM and the second kicks in at high RPM.
It won 'Playboy's Car of the Year' in 1993, beating out high-end European supercars.
The design used 'organic' shapes with almost no straight lines, a style that has aged beautifully.
The 570S was the first model in McLaren's 'Sports Series', designed to be more usable and affordable than their Super Series cars.
It has no active aerodynamics, unlike its big brother the 650S, relying instead on its sculpted shape for downforce.
It features 'Dihedral Doors' that open forward and upward, a McLaren signature.
The 'LT' name stands for Longtail, a reference to McLaren's 1990s F1 GTR race cars.
It features 'top-exit' exhausts that spit blue flames directly behind the driver's head.
The carbon fiber body is 2.9 inches longer than the standard 570S to improve aerodynamic balance at high speed.
The front end was redesigned to look like the McLaren P1 hypercar.
It features a 'ProActive Chassis Control' system that replaces traditional anti-roll bars with a complex hydraulic setup.
The Spider version is only 88 lbs heavier than the coupe, with no loss in structural rigidity.
The 675LT was the first modern 'Longtail' McLaren, focused purely on track performance and driver engagement.
It produced 666 metric horsepower, a number that led some to nickname it 'The Devil's Car'.
McLaren removed the air conditioning as standard to save weight, though most customers added it back as a no-cost option.
The headlights are actually hollow air intakes that channel air into the radiators.
It features a 'Variable Drift Control' that allows the driver to select exactly how many degrees of slide the car allows.
The interior has a 'Folding Driver Display' that flips down into a slim strip to show only essential racing info.
It features unique glazed 'flying buttresses' that provide high visibility while maintaining a sleek silhouette.
The hard-top roof can be opened or closed in just 11 seconds at speeds up to 31 mph.
It is widely considered the fastest convertible in the world in terms of real-world track performance.
The 765LT is 176 lbs lighter than the already featherweight 720S.
It features a quad-exit titanium exhaust system that is 40% lighter than standard steel exhausts.
Only 765 units were produced globally, making it highly exclusive.
The Artura is McLaren's first series-production High-Performance Hybrid (HPH).
Its V6 engine has a 120-degree angle, allowing the turbochargers to be placed in a 'Hot V' for better response.
It uses an 'E-diff' (electronic differential), which is integrated into the transmission to precisely manage power.
Designed as a grand tourer, it has enough cargo space (across two trunks) for a full set of golf clubs.
It is the longest car in the modern McLaren lineup to accommodate more interior comfort and storage.
The interior uses unique 'NASA-developed' fabrics that are scratch-resistant and easy to clean.
The first car designed and built entirely by McLaren since the legendary F1.
The name 'MP4' was a prefix used for all McLaren Formula 1 cars since 1981.
It used a unique brake-steer system (which used the rear brakes to pull the car into corners) that was later banned in F1.
One of the 'Holy Trinity' of hypercars alongside the Porsche 918 and Ferrari LaFerrari.
It produces so much downforce (600kg) that the rear wing can literally flatten itself at high speeds to prevent the suspension from breaking.
Only 375 units were produced, and they are now worth triple their original price.
Named after legendary F1 driver Ayrton Senna, who won three world titles with McLaren.
The design was described by McLaren as 'brutal' because it was entirely dictated by aerodynamics rather than beauty.
It features optional transparent glass panels in the lower half of the doors so the driver can see the road passing under them.
The last C-Class AMG to feature a V8 engine before switching to a 4-cylinder hybrid.
It features dynamic engine mounts that stiffen or soften depending on the driving conditions.
The 'S' version comes standard with an electronic limited-slip differential for better corner exit speeds.
The Edition 1 was a limited-launch model inspired by the design of the DTM (German Touring Car) race cars.
It features unique matte grey paint with yellow racing stripes across the hood, roof, and sides.
It came standard with racing bucket seats and ceramic high-performance brakes.
The C43 was originally marketed as the 'AMG Sport' before being promoted to a full AMG model.
It uses a rear-biased all-wheel-drive system (31/69 split) to maintain a sporty driving feel.
The current generation's 4-cylinder engine uses an electric exhaust gas turbocharger derived from Formula 1.
Introduced a dedicated 'Drift Mode' which disconnects the front wheels to make the car purely rear-wheel drive.
Despite being a luxury sedan, it can accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in just 3.0 seconds.
The engine features 'cylinder deactivation', turning into a 4-cylinder at cruise to save fuel.
Despite its brick-like aerodynamics, the G 63 can reach 60 mph in under 4.5 seconds.
It is famous for its side-exit exhaust pipes that exit just ahead of the rear wheels.
Every G-Wagon is hand-built in Graz, Austria, a tradition that has continued since 1979.
It was the first sports car developed entirely in-house by Mercedes-AMG.
The engine uses a 'Hot V' configuration, where the two turbochargers are placed inside the V-shape of the cylinders.
It uses a dry-sump lubrication system, allowing the engine to be mounted very low in the chassis.

regionalNames: The Beast of the Green Hell
funFacts: The launch color was a unique matte green called 'AMG Green Hell Magno', a tribute to the Nürburgring track.
It features active underbody aerodynamics—a carbon fiber panel that lowers at high speeds to create a 'Venturi effect'.
It has a 9-stage traction control dial on the dashboard, taken directly from the AMG GT3 race car.
The 170V was Mercedes-Benz's most important model before and after World War II.
The 'V' in the name stands for 'Vorne' (Front), as the engine was in the front (there was also a rear-engine 170H).
It was one of the only cars to resume production in Germany immediately after the war ended in 1946.
Part of the 'Ponton' family, which were the first Mercedes models with a modern integrated body design.
The W121 190 was a more affordable version of the luxury 220 series.
It was famously durable, often used as taxis in Germany and Africa for decades.
Launched as an affordable alternative to the legendary 300 SL 'Gullwing'.
It was designed by Max Hoffman, the same US importer who suggested the 300 SL and Porsche Speedster.
It featured an elegant, classic silhouette that made it a favorite among Hollywood stars like Grace Kelly.
The 190SL and 300SL were displayed together at the 1954 New York Auto Show, a major milestone for Mercedes in America.
The engine was based on the 300SL's straight-six, but with two cylinders removed.
Over 25,000 units were built, making it much more common than its exotic 300SL sibling.
The 220 S was part of the 'Ponton' family, featuring the first Mercedes-Benz monocoque body design.
It used a complex 'Hydrak' automatic clutch system that allowed for gear changes without a clutch pedal.
The interior featured extensive use of high-quality wood veneer, setting the standard for German luxury cabins.
Known as the 'Pagoda' because of the unique concave shape of its removable hardtop roof.
It was the first sports car in the world to feature a 'safety body' with rigid passenger cells and crumple zones.
Formula 1 champion Juan Manuel Fangio famously owned a 230 SL as his personal road car.
The 280 SL was the final and most powerful evolution of the W113 'Pagoda' series.
It featured four-wheel disc brakes, a major performance upgrade over many contemporary sports cars.
It is one of the most reliable and usable classic cars today, often used by collectors for long-distance rallies.
The 'Gullwing' doors were a necessity, not a style choice, because the high tubular spaceframe chassis left no room for traditional doors.
It was the first production car to feature direct fuel injection, which was derived from Messerschmitt fighter plane engines.
At launch, it was the fastest production car in the world, capable of 163 mph (263 km/h).
It was born when an engineer decided to see if the massive V8 from the 600 limo would fit into the smaller S-Class body.
It was the fastest four-door sedan in the world at the time, earning it the nickname 'The Executive Hot Rod'.
It featured self-leveling air suspension, which provided a cloud-like ride despite the car's massive power.
The Pullman was the extra-long-wheelbase version, featuring four doors and rear-facing middle seats.
Almost every function in the car (windows, seats, sunroof, doors) was operated by a silent 3,200 psi hydraulic system.
It was the preferred car for celebrities like John Lennon, Elvis Presley, and dozens of world dictators.

engine: Various options including V8 and I6
funFacts: Originally developed as a military vehicle at the suggestion of the Shah of Iran.
It is one of the few vehicles in the world with three fully locking differentials as standard equipment.
The door handles and the sound of the doors closing have remained identical since the car's launch in 1979.
The S-Class is widely regarded as the 'tech preview' for the rest of the car world; features like ABS and Airbags debuted here.
The name comes from 'Sonderklasse,' which means 'Special Class' in German.
It is consistently the best-selling luxury sedan in the world.
The SLS was the first Mercedes-Benz designed entirely from scratch by the AMG performance division.
It featured modern 'Gullwing' doors that used explosive bolts to blow the doors off in case the car rolled over.
The engine was the most powerful naturally aspirated production V8 in the world at the time of its release.
The MGB was one of the first cars in the world to feature controlled 'crumple zones' to protect occupants in a crash.
It used a unibody construction, which was very advanced for a sports car in the early 1960s.
Over 500,000 units were built, making it one of the most successful sports cars in British history.
The 'S' was developed by racing legend John Cooper, who saw the potential for the Mini to be a giant-killer on the track.
The classic Mini Cooper S won the Monte Carlo Rally four times (though it was disqualified once on a technicality).
Modern BMW-built MINI Cooper S models use a 'Go-Kart Feeling' marketing slogan to reference the car's sharp handling.
The Evo X was the first in the series to move away from the legendary 4G63 engine to an all-aluminum block.
